Under GDPR, Data Controller The relationship between the Data Controller and GDPR signifies the Data Controller's comprehensive obligations and responsibilities under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). Data controllers are mandated to comply with GDPR regulations concerning the processing of personal data. This includes adherence to obligations related to transparency and the provision of information to data subjects, specifically as outlined in articles such as Article 13 and Article 14 (including Article 14(3)). Furthermore, the GDPR applies to data controllers who determine the means for processing personal data, even for personal or household activities. Data controllers are also responsible for performing specific actions in the event of personal data breaches, and the GDPR places the burden of proof on them to demonstrate lawful and transparent data collection and processing practices. Article 26 is also highlighted as a relevant obligation for data controllers under GDPR.<SEP>A data controller monitoring data subjects in the Union is subject to the GDPR per Article 3(2)(b).<SEP>Controllers not established in the EU are required to comply with the GDPR if their processing targets individuals in the Union as per Article 3(2).<SEP>Data Controllers must comply with GDPR for processing activities and data exchanges.<SEP>The data controller must demonstrate compliance with the GDPR.<SEP>The GDPR prescribes requirements and obligations for data controllers regarding data subject rights.<SEP>A Data Controller must ensure compliance with GDPR requirements for personal data provision. GDPR.
Under GDPR, Digital Markets Act (DMA) The DMA is intended to address the erosion of market contestability caused by data accumulation. Market Contestability.

Supervisory Authorities.

Under GDPR, Business User A business user may access performance data for providing customer support. Performance Data.
Under GDPR, Codes Of Conduct The Commission encourages the drawing up of codes of conduct. The Commission.
Under GDPR, Internal Market The Internal Market is a core component within the Union, whose proper functioning requires unrestricted free movement of personal data. Union.
Under GDPR, New Technologies Personal data processing operations may involve using new technologies. Personal Data Processing.
Under GDPR, Online Advertising Services Tracking technology is used by online advertising services to collect personal data. Tracking Technology.
Under GDPR, Board Information is exchanged by electronic means between supervisory authorities and the Board. Electronic Means.
Under GDPR, Controlling Undertaking A controlling undertaking can exert dominant influence through financial participation. Financial Participation.
Under GDPR, GDPR The GDPR uses the concept of an undertaking for imposing administrative fines. Undertaking.
Under GDPR, Consent End users provide consent for the processing of their personal data.<SEP>Providers of apps or app stores seek valid consent from end users to process data. End Users.

Under GDPR, Personal Data Profiling involves the automated processing of personal data to evaluate personal aspects and make predictions about individuals.<SEP>Profiling must be carried out on personal data.<SEP>Profiling involves the analysis of personal data to determine aspects of an individual’s personality or behavior.<SEP>Profiling can involve the use of personal data.<SEP>Personal data is used as input for profiling.<SEP>Profiling involves the automated processing and use of personal data.<SEP>Data subjects have the right not to be subject to decisions based solely on profiling using personal data. Profiling.
Under GDPR, Digital Markets Act (DMA) The DMA aims to address gatekeepers’ data-driven advantages. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Data Portability Online choice architecture affects data portability. Online Choice Architecture.
Under GDPR, End User Account Settings Gatekeepers prompt end users and record their preferences via signed-in end users’ account settings. Gatekeeper.
Under GDPR, Digital Markets Act (DMA) The High-Level Group (HLG) for the DMA provides advice and expertise to the Commission in areas within its members' competences. High-Level Group (HLG).
Under GDPR, Delivery Service Identification details may be cross-used by a delivery service without consent, under Article 5(2), point (c) DMA. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Commission The Commission is responsible for interpreting provisions of the DMA.<SEP>The Commission conducts proceedings and investigations under the DMA. DMA.
Under GDPR, Direct Marketing The GDPR sets regulations for the processing of personal data in direct marketing. GDPR.

Under GDPR, Code of Conduct A Code of Conduct must facilitate the effective application of the GDPR by stipulating its sector-specific application.<SEP>The Code of Conduct contributes to the proper application of the GDPR and ensures compliance with its provisions. GDPR.
Under GDPR, Controller Controllers that are part of a group of undertakings may have a legitimate interest in transmitting personal data within the group. Group of Undertakings.

(b) the data transfers or set of transfers, including the categories of personal data, the type of processing and its purposes, the type of data subjects affected and the identification of the third country or countries in question; (c) their legally binding nature, both internally and externally; (d) the application of the general data protection principles, in particular purpose limitation, data minimisation, limited storage periods, data quality, data protection by design and by default, legal basis for processing, processing of special categories of personal data, measures to ensure data security, and the requirements in respect of onward transfers to bodies not bound by the binding corporate rules; (e) the rights of data subjects in regard to processing and the means to exercise those rights, including the right not to be subject to decisions based solely on automated processing, including profiling in accordance with Article 22, the right to lodge a complaint with the competent supervisory authority and before the competent courts of the Member States in accordance with Article 79, and to obtain redress and, where appropriate, compensation for a breach of the binding corporate rules; (f) the acceptance by the controller or processor established on the territory of a Member State of liability for any breaches of the binding corporate rules by any member concerned not established in the Union; the controller or the processor shall be exempt from that liability, in whole or in part, only if it proves that that member is not responsible for the event giving rise to the damage; (g) how the information on the binding corporate rules, in particular on the provisions referred to in points (d), (e) and (f) of this paragraph is provided to the data subjects in addition to Articles 13 and 14; (i) the complaint procedures; (j) the mechanisms within the group of undertakings, or group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity for ensuring the verification of compliance with the binding corporate rules. Such mechanisms shall include data protection audits and methods for ensuring corrective actions to protect the rights of the data subject. Results of such verification should be communicated to the person or entity referred to in point (h) and to the board of the controlling undertaking of a group of undertakings, or of the group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity, and should be available upon request to the competent supervisory authority; (k) the mechanisms for reporting and recording changes to the rules and reporting those changes to the supervisory authority; (l) the cooperation mechanism with the supervisory authority to ensure compliance by any member of the group of undertakings, or group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity, in particular by making available to the supervisory authority the results of verifications of the measures referred to in point (j); (m) the mechanisms for reporting to the competent supervisory authority any legal requirements to which a member of the group of undertakings, or group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity is subject in a third country which are likely to have a substantial adverse effect on the guarantees provided by the binding corporate rules; and (n) the appropriate data protection training to personnel having permanent or regular access to personal data. 4.5.2016 L 119/63 Official Journal of the European Union EN 3. [Source: joint_com-edpb_gls_interplay_dma_gdpr_for_public_consultation_en] "to provide effective interoperability. Any such collection and exchange of the personal data of end users shall fully comply with Regulation (EU) 2016/679 and Directive 2002/58/EC.” Lastly, Article 7(9) DMA provides that “The gatekeeper shall not be prevented from taking measures to ensure that third-party providers of NIICS 154 For the definition of NIICS, see Article 2(9) DMA, referring to the definition contained in Article 2(7) of Directive (EU) 2018/1972. 155 Recital 64. 156 According to Article 2(29) DMA, “‘interoperability’ means the ability to exchange information and mutually use the information which has been exchanged through interfaces or other solutions, so that all elements of hardware or software work with other hardware and software and with users in all the ways in which they are intended to function.” 157 Article 7(4) DMA. 158 Recital 64 DMA. requesting interoperability do not endanger the integrity, security and privacy of its services, provided that such measures are strictly necessary and proportionate and are duly justified.” 195. To establish interoperability, the duties and responsibilities of two parties – i.e., the gatekeeper and the NIICS provider requesting interoperability – should be considered. 196. On the side of the NIICS provider requesting interoperability, the provider will need to comply and implement the technical details and general terms and conditions for interoperability set out by the reference offer of the gatekeeper, including in what concerns personal data processing that is necessary to enable interoperability. From the moment in which interoperability is established, the NIICS provider requesting interoperability is responsible for complying with Union data protection law in relation to the personal data it accesses (e.g., when providing the NIICS to the end user that decides to make use of the interoperable basic functionalities). 197. Pursuant to Article 7(4) DMA, the gatekeeper is required to draft and publish a reference offer with technical details, general terms and conditions on interoperability. The Reference Offer should ensure effective interoperability with the basic functionalities listed in Article 7(2) DMA. Such reference offer and more broadly the gatekeeper’s measures to ensure compliance with Article 7 DMA should ensure full compliance with Union data protection law,159 including that only personal data that is strictly necessary for provision of effective interoperability is processed in compliance with Article 5(1), point (c) GDPR. 198. The implementation of interoperability by the gatekeeper under Article 7 DMA is very likely to fulfil the criteria for the requirement to carry out a data protection impact assessment under Article 35 GDPR160 which includes an assessment of possible risks to the rights and freedoms of data subjects arising from data exchange that is needed for the purpose of ensuring interoperability, as well as appropriate, timely and effective measures to tackle the risks identified. 199. Under Article 7(8) DMA, the gatekeeper has to ensure full compliance with Union data protection legislation when sharing personal data with providers of NIICS requesting interoperability. In particular, the gatekeeper is obliged to collect and share only personal data that is strictly necessary to provide effective interoperability and so in line with GDPR and the ePrivacy Directive. In line with Article 5(1), point (c) GDPR, controllers should consider if the purpose of the processing requires use of personal data or if anonymised data can be used and using the latter where possible. This data minimisation principle is particularly relevant in the case where gatekeepers need to collect additional personal data from end users that the gatekeeper does not use itself when providing its own communication service, for the sole purpose of providing effective interoperability, while meeting all requirements of Article 7 DMA and Union data protection law, in particular to maintain the level of security across interoperable services. 159 Article 8(1) DMA. 160 See Article 29 Data Protection Working Party Guidelines on Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) and determining whether processing is “likely to result in a high risk” for the purposes of Regulation 2016/679 (WP 248 rev.01), As last Revised and Adopted on 4 October 2017, p. 10. Interoperability under article 7 DMA is likely to satisfy more than two of the criteria mentioned, i.e. it relates to data of a highly personal nature (electronic communications whose confidentiality should be protected), is large scale, and relies on innovative technical processes. 200. The other parts of this section look at the interplay between Union data protection law and the DMA, in relation to the categories of personal data that are necessary for the provision of interoperability (cf. section 7.1), personal data processing to preserve the level of security across interoperable services (section 7.2) and other situations where personal data processing may take place in the context of Article 7 interoperability (section 7.3). 7.1 Categories of personal data necessary to ensure interoperability 201. Personal data needed to ensure interoperability can be separated in the following three major categories: a. the message content themselves that would be exchanged between servers of the gatekeeper and those of the other providers. b. the personal data that is needed to ensure technical aspects of interoperability between the NIICS of the gatekeeper and of the provider requesting interoperability, including to ensure that end users of the different NIICS can address each other and communicate."

Bundeskartellamt (ECLI:EU:C:2023:537), para. 109. 124 See further the section 7.1 on “transmission to competent authorities” below in these guidelines. 125 For more details, see para. 26 of the present guidelines. 30 Adopted - version for public consultation 109. According to Recital 47 GDPR, the processing of personal data for direct marketing purposes may be regarded as carried out for a legitimate interest. Direct marketing is not defined in the GDPR. However, CJEU case law suggests that personalised advertising could be considered a form of direct marketing.126 Moreover, the CJEU has interpreted the notion of communication for direct marketing purposes under the ePrivacy Directive,127 which is closely linked to the GDPR128 and regulates the sending of direct marketing communications.129 In particular, the CJEU found that to assess whether a communication is made for direct marketing purposes it must be ascertained whether such a communication pursues a commercial purpose and is addressed directly and individually to a consumer.130 In this respect the CJEU found that it is irrelevant whether the advertising at issue is addressed to a predetermined and individually identified recipient or is sent on a mass, random basis to multiple recipients.131 What matters is that there is a communication for a commercial purpose, which reaches, directly and individually, a consumer.132 In this regard, it should be noted that the Court found, for instance, that, based on these criteria, advertising consisting in displaying advertising banners, disguised as emails, into the private email inboxes of the users of an email service – funded by advertising and provided for free to users – is a form of direct marketing (even though such advertising does not entail the sending of an email to a specific consumer).133 This understanding of the notion of direct marketing purposes may in principle be used by way of analogy to understand its meaning also within the GDPR. This is because the interpretation of a provision of EU law requires that account be taken not only of its wording and the objectives it pursues, but also of its context and the provisions of EU law as a whole. 134 110. The fact that Recital 47 GDPR states that the processing of personal data for direct marketing purposes may be carried out to fulfil a legitimate interest does not mean that direct marketing always constitutes a legitimate interest, and that it is automatically possible to rely on Article 6(1)(f) GDPR to engage in all kinds of direct marketing activities. 111. For some cases of direct marketing, a different legal basis – such as consent – may be required, thus precluding the use of legitimate interest as a legal basis in this context, as explained below. 112. Furthermore, it has to be borne in mind that, as outlined above, reliance on legitimate interest requires that three cumulative conditions be fulfilled, namely, first, the pursuit of a legitimate interest by the controller or by a third party; second, the need to process personal data for the purposes of the legitimate interests pursued; and third, that the interests or fundamental freedoms and rights of the person concerned by the relevant processing do not take precedence over the legitimate interest of the controller or of a third party (see further Chapter II above in these guidelines). 135 It follows from this that the processing of personal data for direct marketing purposes cannot be based on Article 6(1)(f) GDPR if these 126 CJEU, judgment of 4 July 2023, Case C-252/21, Meta v. The task "ob Lehrender in CMS verfügbar ist prüfen" verifies teacher availability in the CMS, accessing teacher personal data.
The task "Lehrenden der Veranstaltung zuteilen" assigns teachers to a course, directly handling teacher personal data.
The task "aktuelle Veranstaltungs-Liste generieren" generates a list of events, which typically includes teacher names and contact details, thus processing personal data.
The task "welche Änderungen an der Veranstaltung notwendig sind prüfen" checks required changes to a course, involving teacher data.
The task "Änderungen vornehmen" makes changes to a course, which includes updating teacher information, processing personal data.
The task "ob neue Veranstaltungen angelegt werden müssen prüfen" (Lehrender lane) checks for new events, accessing teacher data, processing personal data.
The task "ob eine Änderung für eine vorhandenen Veranstaltung notwendig ist prüfen" (Lehrender lane) evaluates teacher changes, processing personal data.
The task "eingeschränkt Änderungen vornehmen" performs restricted changes to a course, involving teacher data, thus processing personal data.
The task "Änderungen, die er nicht selbst vornehmen darf melden" reports changes that cannot be made, which requires accessing teacher data, processing personal data.

ragPromptContext:
[concept] Article 7 DMA — Article 7 of the DMA requires gatekeepers designated in relation to certain criteria.
Article 7 DMA requires gatekeepers to offer interoperability to alternative service providers for number-independent interpersonal communications services. It also addresses privacy and data protection concerns.
Article 7 DMA is a legal provision under the Digital Markets Act concerning interoperability requests.

[organization] Official Authorities — Official Authorities are entities that process personal data for aims laid down by constitutional law or international public law, particularly for officially recognized religious associations.
[organization] Union (European Union) — The Union refers to the European Union, which has an important public interest.
[person] Auditor — An auditor, who can be mandated by the controller, conducts inspections/audits to verify compliance.
[person] Joint Representative — An individual or entity appointed within a Member State when more than one supervisory authority is responsible for monitoring the application of the regulation.
[event] Merger — A change in legal and factual circumstances, such as the combining of the third-party undertaking with another company.

[concept] Restrictions — Limitations on the scope of obligations and rights provided in certain GDPR articles, applied under specific conditions listed in Article 23 GDPR.
Limitations applied to the scope of obligations and rights provided for in the GDPR, permissible under certain conditions detailed in Article 23.
Restrictions are limitations that can be imposed by Union or Member State law on specific principles and rights related to personal data processing, provided they are necessary and proportionate in a democratic society.
Legislative measures that may limit the scope of obligations and rights related to data protection under certain conditions.

[organization] Lead Authority — The lead authority is responsible for receiving notifications in the case of a cross-border personal data breach.
The Lead Authority is the relevant supervisory authority in the case of a cross-border personal data breach.
A supervisory authority identified to handle processing activities, especially for multinational companies or groups of undertakings based on decision-making centers.
The Lead Authority is a supervisory authority competent to adopt binding decisions in cases where a controller or processor is established in multiple Member States or substantially affects data subjects in more than one Member State.
The Lead Authority is a supervisory authority competent to adopt binding decisions and coordinate with other supervisory authorities in cases involving more than one Member State.
The lead authority is a supervisory authority whose draft decisions can be subject to objections from other supervisory authorities, as per Article 60(4).

[organization] Lead Supervisory Authority — The Lead Supervisory Authority (LSA) is a central concept within data protection under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), specifically designed for cross-border processing activities. Also recognized as the "one-stop-shop" mechanism, the LSA is the supervisory authority situated in the Member State of a controller's or processor's main establishment. Its primary role is to lead GDPR enforcement for cases that span multiple Member States, acting as the sole point of contact for the controller or processor in such multi-jurisdictional data protection scenarios. The LSA has a leading function in cooperation procedures, explicitly mandated to collaborate with other Concerned Supervisory Authorities (CSAs). Its responsibilities include overseeing data protection operations, conducting investigations, and sharing relevant information with other CSAs. The LSA is also tasked with preparing and submitting draft decisions under Articles 60 and 62, striving for consensus. It plays a crucial role in the Article 60 procedure. Furthermore, the LSA is responsible for handling objections raised by other CSAs. Should a dispute arise where the LSA does not follow a relevant and reasoned objection, or if it deems an objection not relevant or reasoned, it refers the matter to the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) for resolution. The LSA also conducts joint operations, facilitates information exchange, and serves as the primary authority a controller must notify in the event of a cross-border processing breach. As specified in GDPR Article 56, the LSA is a specific supervisory authority for transnational code members, underscoring its integral role in managing cross-border data protection and ensuring compliance, including taking measures intended to produce legal effects like administrative fines.
The Lead Supervisory Authority is the primary interlocutor for the Commission in cases where a gatekeeper has a "main establishment" under the GDPR, responsible for cooperating with concerned supervisory authorities.

[method] Anonymisation — Anonymisation is a fundamental process and set of techniques designed to transform personal data in such a way that the data subject can no longer be identified, either directly or indirectly. This alteration ensures that the data cannot be linked back to an individual, even with reasonable effort. It is employed when personal data is deleted or anonymized following the withdrawal of consent, acting as a crucial safeguard in data processing, particularly for research purposes where the objective can be achieved without handling identifiable data. This process involves carefully processing data, such as location information, to prevent re-identification, a task that is complex and necessitates transparency regarding the methodologies used. The goal of anonymisation extends beyond simply removing identifying information to also preserving the quality and usefulness of the data for its intended purpose. It is a preferred solution and safeguard, recognized by regulations like the Digital Markets Act (DMA), specifically Article 6(11), and the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), which require the rendering of personal data anonymous to protect end-user privacy. In essence, anonymisation serves as a vital method for making shared personal data suitable for use while upholding the privacy of individuals.
Anonymization is a method by which shared data that constitutes personal data is processed to remove identifying information.
Anonymisation is a method of altering personal data so that the data subject is no longer identifiable, with the goal of preserving data quality and usefulness.
Anonymisation is the process of altering personal data to prevent direct or indirect identification of end users, required for data shared under Article 6(11) DMA.
Anonymisation is the process of rendering personal data anonymous to protect end-user privacy, as required by DMA and GDPR.
Under GDPR, Article 7 GDPR Article 7 is part of the GDPR and sets out conditions for valid consent. GDPR.
Under GDPR, Official Authorities Official Authorities process Personal Data for aims of religious associations. Personal Data.

Under GDPR, Lead Supervisory Authority The Lead Supervisory Authority cooperates with other Supervisory Authorities Concerned, exchanging information and seeking consensus on draft decisions. Supervisory Authorities Concerned.
Under GDPR, Anonymisation Gatekeepers are responsible for selecting the appropriate anonymisation methods for end-user data. Gatekeeper.

Under GDPR, Controller The relationship between a Controller and a Lead Supervisory Authority is central to the oversight of cross-border data processing, particularly under regulations such as the GDPR. The Lead Supervisory Authority is identified for a Controller primarily based on its central administration or the location where decisions concerning processing are made. In situations involving both a Controller and a Processor, the Lead Supervisory Authority for the Controller typically assumes the role for both, as outlined in Recital 36. Furthermore, the supervisory authority of a Member State where a Controller's main establishment is located often acts as the competent Lead Supervisory Authority. This authority is competent to supervise cross-border processing activities conducted by a Controller. A key responsibility of the Controller in this relationship is to notify the Lead Supervisory Authority in the event of breaches involving cross-border processing. Beyond notification, the Lead Supervisory Authority serves as the sole interlocutor for the Controller regarding cross-border processing, meaning the Controller interacts exclusively with this authority. The Lead Supervisory Authority is responsible for adopting and notifying decisions to the Controller, who, in turn, is obligated to take measures to ensure compliance with these decisions. Lead Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Controller A controller may or may not be established in the Union, which determines certain aspects of this Regulation's applicability. The Union.
Under GDPR, Auditor The processor must make information available and contribute to audits conducted by the auditor. Processor.

Under GDPR, Controller In the context of the GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation), a Controller is defined as the entity that determines the purposes and means of processing personal data. This role is fundamental to the GDPR framework, outlining significant obligations and responsibilities for any party involved in deciding how personal data is processed. Controllers are accountable for ensuring compliance with all GDPR provisions, particularly as detailed in Article 3. The Controller has legal obligations to ensure compliance with the GDPR and is responsible and liable under its provisions, with these obligations often referred to as "GDPR controller obligations." The responsibilities of a Controller include applying GDPR rules to data processing, adhering to foundational principles like lawfulness and fairness (Article 5(1)), and fulfilling transparency obligations. Specific duties extend to maintaining records of processing activities (Article 30), ensuring the security of processing (Article 32), conducting data protection impact assessments (Article 35), and engaging in prior consultation (Article 36) for managing risks. Controllers must also comply with regulations regarding data storage periods, implement appropriate technical and organizational measures, and adhere to specific legal bases, such as legitimate interest, for processing data. The GDPR places a significant burden of proof on the Controller, requiring them to demonstrate "compelling legitimate grounds for the processing" when applicable. They must ensure consent mechanisms meet GDPR standards and act upon withdrawal requests. Compliance with obligations related to accountability, engaging processors, and maintaining data security is paramount. Regarding data breaches, the GDPR mandates that controllers implement appropriate security measures, ensure compliance with processing principles, and adhere to relevant provisions for breach notification, as specified in Article 60(10). Unless a breach is unlikely to result in a risk to individuals' rights and freedoms, the Controller is required to notify the competent supervisory authority and, in certain cases, the affected individuals. The GDPR further defines notification and communication requirements for Controllers regarding breaches, and they must retain documentation in accordance with Article 33(5) of the GDPR. The GDPR also places the burden of proof regarding objections on the Controller. Supervisory Authorities are empowered to take enforcement action, including imposing fines, for any infringement of GDPR provisions resulting from a Controller's actions. The ability for data controllers to obtain GDPR certifications further underscores their pivotal role and accountability within the regulatory framework, though some Controllers currently face broad and systemic issues in compliance with the GDPR. The GDPR recognizes that Controllers may not have all information immediately and allows for phased notification.<SEP>Controllers must comply with the provisions of the GDPR, including those related to information provision.<SEP>The controller must comply with GDPR requirements, including those related to transparency and notification.<SEP>A controller's responsibilities are defined under the GDPR. GDPR.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller must cease other processing activities while retaining personal data when a restriction is in place. Right to Restriction of Processing.
Under GDPR, Lead Authority The Lead Authority is a type of Supervisory Authority that coordinates with other Supervisory Authorities in decision-making processes. Supervisory Authority.

Under GDPR, Competent Data Protection Supervisory Authorities The European Commission coordinates, cooperates, and consults with competent data protection supervisory authorities. European Commission.
Under GDPR, Data Protection Training Appropriate data protection training is required for personnel having permanent or regular access to personal data. Personnel.
Under GDPR, Gatekeepers Gatekeepers should keep an internal list of all categories of data that can be ported under Article 6(9) DMA. Internal List of Data Categories.
Under GDPR, Controller Controllers should verify the identity of a data subject requests access, particularly in context of online services. Online Services.
Under GDPR, Merger The enforceability of measures imposed by the implementing act should remain even after a merger of the third party undertaking. Third-Party Undertaking.
Under GDPR, CPS A hyperlink to the dashboard for data recipients may be included in the CPS's privacy notice. Privacy Notice.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Restriction of processing applies to personal data. Restriction of Processing.
Under GDPR, Commission M. Schulz, as President of the European Parliament, represents one of the parties for which the Regulation was done. M. Schulz.
Under GDPR, Economic Union Regulation (EU) 2016/679 is intended to contribute to the accomplishment of an economic union. Regulation (EU) 2016/679.

Under GDPR, Controller The relation "(Controller, Personal Data)" signifies that the Controller is the entity that determines the purposes and means of processing personal data. This involves significant authority and responsibility over the entire lifecycle of personal data, encompassing decisions related to its processing, protection, management, and transference. The Controller is explicitly responsible for establishing the retention period for documentation containing personal data and makes choices regarding its deletion or return. A core obligation of the Controller is to ensure compliance with data protection regulations and to uphold data subject rights, including guaranteeing data accuracy and facilitating the exercise of those rights. The Controller is always required to have an appropriate lawful ground for the processing of personal data, and while explicit consent may be obtained, it is not always the sole lawful basis, especially when processing is essential for a contract. The Controller is also responsible for the overall protection and security of personal data, which includes managing and responding to any data breaches, and applying appropriate technical and organizational measures to safeguard it. This responsibility extends to implementing measures to minimize the processing of personal data. In addition to determining purposes and means, the Controller collects, processes, and stores personal data. The Controller may also determine its identification requirements and, in the EU, has the capability to make personal data accessible to a third-country authority, and may specifically process data indicating criminal acts when considered within their legitimate interest. Persons under the direct authority of the Controller are authorized to process personal data. The Controller is ultimately responsible and liable for any processing of personal data, including its transferring. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Processor A processor may or may not be established in the Union, which determines certain aspects of this Regulation's applicability. The Union.

Under GDPR, GDPR The Lead Supervisory Authority leads the enforcement of the GDPR for cross-border processing activities.<SEP>The GDPR provides for shared responsibility in monitoring its application, influencing the LSA's position. Lead Supervisory Authority.

Under GDPR, Controller The controller must implement restriction of processing under specified conditions and verify data accuracy.<SEP>The controller must communicate any restriction of processing. Restriction of Processing.
Under GDPR, Direct Marketing This judgment provides legal precedent for the notion of direct marketing. StWL Städtische Werke Lauf a.d. Pegnitz v eprimo GmbH, C-102/20.

Under GDPR, Consent Pursuant to Directive 95/46/EC Processing based on consent under Directive 95/46/EC does not require new consent if it aligns with this Regulation's conditions. Processing Already Under Way.
Under GDPR, Data Protection Supervisory Authorities The lead authority cooperates with other supervisory authorities concerned to adopt binding decisions. Lead Authority.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Personal data can be processed for the purposes of planning and organisation of work. Planning and Organisation of Work.

Under GDPR, Controller The controller provides contact details for the controller's representative.<SEP>The record of processing activities for a controller may include details of the controller's representative. Controller's Representative.
Under GDPR, Controller Or Processor A data protection authority examines whether a controller’s or processor’s conduct is compliant with the GDPR. General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).
Under GDPR, Commission The Commission considers developments in information technology when proposing amendments to the Regulation. Information Technology.

The shared data that constitutes personal data has to be anonymised. The Guidelines clarify the objective of Article 6(11) DMA to foster contestability in the online search engine market and how to achieve effective anonymisation of shared search data while taking into account such objectives. Article 7 DMA requires gatekeepers designated in relation to their number-independent interpersonal communications services to offer interoperability to alternative service providers requesting it. The Guidelines elaborate on the requirements of Article 7 that are relevant from the perspective of privacy and data protection law. Most notably, they recall that gatekeepers should comply with data minimisation and other GDPR principles when making their services interoperable, and mention necessary, proportionate, and justified measures that a gatekeeper may apply to ensure that third-party service providers requesting interoperability do not endanger the integrity, security and privacy of its services. The Guidelines also pronounce on coordination, cooperation and consultation between the European Commission as sole enforcer of the DMA and competent data protection supervisory authorities as enforcers of the GDPR. [Source: General_Data_Protection_Regulation_en] ". Article 18 Right to restriction of processing 1. The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller restriction of processing where one of the following applies: (a) the accuracy of the personal data is contested by the data subject, for a period enabling the controller to verify the accuracy of the personal data; (b) the processing is unlawful and the data subject opposes the erasure of the personal data and requests the restriction of their use instead; (c) the controller no longer needs the personal data for the purposes of the processing, but they are required by the data subject for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims; (d) the data subject has objected to processing pursuant to Article 21(1) pending the verification whether the legitimate grounds of the controller override those of the data subject. 2. Where processing has been restricted under paragraph 1, such personal data shall, with the exception of storage, only be processed with the data subject's consent or for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims or for the protection of the rights of another natural or legal person or for reasons of important public interest of the Union or of a Member State. 4.5.2016 L 119/44 Official Journal of the European Union EN 3. A data subject who has obtained restriction of processing pursuant to paragraph 1 shall be informed by the controller before the restriction of processing is lifted. Article 19 Notification obligation regarding rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing The controller shall communicate any rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing carried out in accordance with Article 16, Article 17(1) and Article 18 to each recipient to whom the personal data have been disclosed, unless this proves impossible or involves disproportionate effort. The controller shall inform the data subject about those recipients if the data subject requests it. Article 20 Right to data portability 1. The data subject shall have the right to receive the personal data concerning him or her, which he or she has provided to a controller, in a structured, commonly used and machine-readable format and have the right to transmit those data to another controller without hindrance from the controller to which the personal data have been provided, where: (a) the processing is based on consent pursuant to point (a) of Article 6(1) or point (a) of Article 9(2) or on a contract pursuant to point (b) of Article 6(1); and (b) the processing is carried out by automated means. 2. In exercising his or her right to data portability pursuant to paragraph 1, the data subject shall have the right to have the personal data transmitted directly from one controller to another, where technically feasible. 3. The exercise of the right referred to in paragraph 1 of this Article shall be without prejudice to Article 17. That right shall not apply to processing necessary for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller. 4. The right referred to in paragraph 1 shall not adversely affect the rights and freedoms of others. Section 4 Right to object and automated individual decision-making Article 21 Right to object 1. The data subject shall have the right to object, on grounds relating to his or her particular situation, at any time to processing of personal data concerning him or her which is based on point (e) or (f) of Article 6(1), including profiling based on those provisions. The controller shall no longer process the personal data unless the controller demonstrates compelling legitimate grounds for the processing which override the interests, rights and freedoms of the data subject or for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims. 2. Where personal data are processed for direct marketing purposes, the data subject shall have the right to object at any time to processing of personal data concerning him or her for such marketing, which includes profiling to the extent that it is related to such direct marketing. 3. Where the data subject objects to processing for direct marketing purposes, the personal data shall no longer be processed for such purposes. 4.5.2016 L 119/45 Official Journal of the European Union EN 4. At the latest at the time of the first communication with the data subject, the right referred to in paragraphs 1 and 2 shall be explicitly brought to the attention of the data subject and shall be presented clearly and separately from any other information. 5. In the context of the use of information society services, and notwithstanding Directive 2002/58/EC, the data subject may exercise his or her right to object by automated means using technical specifications. 6. Where personal data are processed for scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes pursuant to Article 89(1), the data subject, on grounds relating to his or her particular situation, shall have the right to object to processing of personal data concerning him or her, unless the processing is necessary for the performance of a task carried out for reasons of public interest. Article 22 Automated individual decision-making, including profiling 1. The data subject shall have the right not to be subject to a decision based solely on automated processing, including profiling, which produces legal effects concerning him or her or similarly significantly affects him or her. 2. Paragraph 1 shall not apply if the decision: (a) is necessary for entering into, or performance of, a contract between the data subject and a data controller; (b) is authorised by Union or"

[Source: edpb_guidelines_202209_personal_data_breach_notification_v2.0_en] "causes, what took place and the personal data affected. It should also include the effects and consequences of the breach, along with the remedial action taken by the controller. 124. The GDPR does not specify a retention period for such documentation. Where such records contain personal data, it will be incumbent on the controller to determine the appropriate period of retention in accordance with the principles in relation to the processing of personal data48 and to meet a lawful basis for processing49. It will need to retain documentation in accordance with Article 33(5) 46 ENISA, Recommendations for a methodology of the assessment of severity of personal data breaches, https://www.enisa.europa.eu/publications/dbn-severity 47 The controller may choose to document breaches as part of if its record of processing activities which is maintained pursuant to Article 30 GDPR. A separate register is not required, provided the information relevant to the breach is clearly identifiable as such and can be extracted upon request. 48 See Article 5 GDPR. 49 See Article 6 and also Article 9 GDPR. Adopted 27 GDPR insofar as it may be called to provide evidence of compliance with that Article, or with the accountability principle more generally, to the supervisory authority. Clearly, if the records themselves contain no personal data then the storage limitation principle50 of the GDPR does not apply. 125. In addition to these details, the EDPB recommends that the controller also document its reasoning for the decisions taken in response to a breach. In particular, if a breach is not notified, a justification for that decision should be documented. This should include reasons why the controller considers the breach is unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals 51. Alternatively, if the controller considers that any of the conditions in Article 34(3) GDPR are met, then it should be able to provide appropriate evidence that this is the case. 126. Where the controller does notify a breach to the supervisory authority, but the notification is delayed, the controller must be able to provide reasons for that delay; documentation relating to this could help to demonstrate that the delay in reporting is justified and not excessive. 127. Where the controller communicates a breach to the affected individuals, it should be transparent about the breach and communicate in an effective and timely manner. Accordingly, it would help the controller to demonstrate accountability and compliance by retaining evidence of such communication. 128. To aid compliance with Articles 33 and 34 GDPR, it would be advantageous to both controllers and processors to have a documented notification procedure in place, setting out the process to follow once a breach has been detected, including how to contain, manage and recover the incident, as well as assessing risk, and notifying the breach. In this regard, to show compliance with GDPR it might also be useful to demonstrate that employees have been informed about the existence of such procedures and mechanisms and that they know how to react to breaches. 129. It should be noted that failure to properly document a breach can lead to the supervisory authority exercising its powers under Article 58 GDPR and, or imposing an administrative fine in accordance with Article 83 GDPR. B. Role of the Data Protection Officer 130. A controller or processor may have a Data Protection Officer (DPO)52, either as required by Article 37 GDPR, or voluntarily as a matter of good practice. Article 39 of the GDPR sets a number of mandatory tasks for the DPO, but does not prevent further tasks being allocated by the controller, if appropriate. 131. Of particular relevance to breach notification, the mandatory tasks of the DPO includes, amongst other duties, providing data protection advice and information to the controller or processor, monitoring compliance with the GDPR, and providing advice in relation to DPIAs. The DPO must also cooperate with the supervisory authority and act as a contact point for the supervisory authority and for data subjects. It should also be noted that, when notifying the breach to the supervisory authority, Article 33(3)(b) GDPR requires the controller to provide the name and contact details of its DPO, or other contact point. 132. In terms of documenting breaches, the controller or processor may wish to obtain the opinion of its DPO as to the structure, the setting up and the administration of this documentation. The DPO could also be additionally tasked with maintaining such records. 133. These factors mean that the DPO should play an key role in assisting the prevention of or preparation for a breach by providing advice and monitoring compliance, as well as during a breach 50 See Article 5(1)(e) GDPR. 51 See Recital 85 GDPR. 52 See WP Guidelines on DPOs here: http://ec.europa.eu/newsroom/just/item-detail.cfm?item_id=50083 Adopted 28 (i.e. when notifying the supervisory authority), and during any subsequent investigation by the supervisory authority. In this light, the EDPB recommends that the DPO is promptly informed about the existence of a breach and is involved throughout the breach management and notification process. VI. NOTIFICATION OBLIGATIONS UNDER OTHER LEGAL INSTRUMENTS 134. In addition to, and separate from, the notification and communication of breaches under the GDPR, controllers should also be aware of any requirement to notify security incidents under other associated legislation that may apply to them and whether this may also require them to notify the supervisory authority of a personal data breach at the same"

Reasoning of the LLM

 - **ob neue Veranstaltungen angelegt werden müssen prüfen (sid-F448B14E-6D6A-4AD1-A5F2-11F833232D89)**: The task "ob neue Veranstaltungen angelegt werden müssen prüfen" checks whether new events need to be created, which requires access to teacher and event data, thus processing personal data. - ** ob eine Änderung des Lehrenden in einer vorhandenen Veranstaltung nötig ist prüfen (sid-C4B1896E-4290-4049-9753-3C607BFAAA46)**: The task "ob eine Änderung des Lehrenden in einer vorhandenen Veranstaltung nötig ist prüfen" evaluates if a teacher change is needed, involving teacher personal data. - **Lehrveran-staltung erstellen (sid-3CD2926F-2770-49BB-AB78-E78E12F25367)**: The task "Lehrveranstaltung erstellen" creates a course, which includes teacher information and event details, processing personal data. - **ob Lehrender in CMS verfügbar ist prüfen (sid-83BA7AB7-58E4-4EB1-9F3D-41100124A651)**: The task "ob Lehrender in CMS verfügbar ist prüfen" verifies teacher availability in the CMS, accessing teacher personal data. - **Lehrenden der Veranstaltung zuteilen (sid-24B998D4-5424-41DD-A564-6CEACF2F115D)**: The task "Lehrenden der Veranstaltung zuteilen" assigns teachers to a course, directly handling teacher personal data. - **aktuelle Veranstaltungs-Liste generieren (sid-34FCE951-492D-4098-8450-5EBEF19E80D0)**: The task "aktuelle Veranstaltungs-Liste generieren" generates a list of events, which typically includes teacher names and contact details, thus processing personal data. - **welche Änderungen an der Veranstaltung notwendig sind prüfen (sid-DD117211-5CF0-42FE-9E0D-81E0D98B07D9)**: The task "welche Änderungen an der Veranstaltung notwendig sind prüfen" checks required changes to a course, involving teacher data. - **Änderungen vornehmen (sid-EC199047-4E2E-45A5-9475-EEF8357F1B49)**: The task "Änderungen vornehmen" makes changes to a course, which includes updating teacher information, processing personal data. - **ob neue Veranstaltungen angelegt werden müssen prüfen (sid-8CD2E544-C82F-444A-B20B-7BA424AE43E4)**: The task "ob neue Veranstaltungen angelegt werden müssen prüfen" (Lehrender lane) checks for new events, accessing teacher data, processing personal data. - **ob eine Änderung für eine vorhandenen Veranstaltung notwendig ist prüfen (sid-B1E78F49-99A0-4AFA-8019-A4042F018C63)**: The task "ob eine Änderung für eine vorhandenen Veranstaltung notwendig ist prüfen" (Lehrender lane) evaluates teacher changes, processing personal data. - **eingeschränkt Änderungen vornehmen (sid-FB83D4A4-0C6B-4936-9B1C-F94569A6D745)**: The task "eingeschränkt Änderungen vornehmen" performs restricted changes to a course, involving teacher data, thus processing personal data. - **Änderungen, die er nicht selbst vornehmen darf melden (sid-56B37B82-3EC3-42AA-B7EF-CBF5E67DDF35)**: The task "Änderungen, die er nicht selbst vornehmen darf melden" reports changes that cannot be made, which requires accessing teacher data, processing personal data.

result.reason: The activity "Vorbereitungs-phase" is part of the admission process and involves reviewing application documents that contain personal data such as names, addresses, and academic records, thereby processing personal data.
The activity "Bewerbung bearbeiten" processes applicants’ personal data by reviewing, evaluating, and recording information contained in their applications.
The activity "Studienplatz vergeben" involves deciding on admission and recording the decision, which directly processes personal data of the applicant.
The activity "Bewerber immatrikulieren" creates and stores enrolment records that contain personal data of the student.
The activity "traditionelles Nachrück-verfahren" handles replacement procedures for students, which requires processing personal data such as student identifiers and contact information.
The activity "Bewerbung durchführen" collects and records applicant information, thereby processing personal data.
The activity "Koordinier-ungsphase 1 mit Entscheidungsphase" coordinates admission decisions and handles personal data of applicants during the decision-making process.
The activity "Koordinierungsphase 2" continues coordination of admission processes, involving further handling of applicants’ personal data.
The activity "Clearingphase" finalises and clears admission records, which requires processing personal data of the students.

ragPromptContext: [method] Processing Already Under Way — This refers to data processing activities that commenced before the Regulation's application date and must be brought into conformity within two years.
[organization] Union (European Union) — The Union refers to the European Union, which has an important public interest.
[method] Recruitment — Recruitment is a purpose in the employment context for which specific rules for processing employees' personal data may be provided.
[concept] Education — Education is a social condition that can be studied in correlation with other life conditions through social science research.

[person] Personnel — Individuals who have permanent or regular access to personal data and require training and education on data protection.
Individuals who have permanent or regular access to personal data and require appropriate periodic training and education.
Personnel involved in a job interview process are data subjects who often expect some of their personal data to be shared with job applicants.

[organization] Data Protection Supervisory Authorities — Data Protection Supervisory Authorities are bodies responsible for overseeing data protection, promoting cooperation, and facilitating information exchange and investigations to enforce legislation for personal data protection.
[concept] Coordination — The process of interaction and collaboration between the European Commission and data protection supervisory authorities.
[concept] Publicly Available Communication Services — Services through which personal data can be publicly accessed, from which links, copies, or replications can be erased.
[Unknown] This Regulation

[data] Personal Data — Personal Data is defined as any information related to an identified or identifiable natural person, often referred to as a "data subject." This encompasses a broad range of details, varying in sensitivity, volume, and context, that can be observed, inferred, or collected. Examples include names, identification numbers, online identifiers, age, gender, geographic location, website activity, interests, sociographic information, behavior, and pictorial or audio-visual data. It can be directly or indirectly identifiable and exist in physical forms, such as paper. Additional examples include technical interoperability data, message content, search queries, language, content preferences, topics of interest, ranking, query, click, and view data. Specific health data like fitness and calorie intake, and information from connected vehicles pertaining to drivers and passengers, also fall under this definition. Certain categories of Personal Data, such as medical history, clinical treatment, physiological or biomedical state, racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religion, health status, sexual orientation, genetic data, biometric data, and data concerning criminal convictions and offenses, are considered special categories that require specific protection. Personal Data also includes information concerning natural persons collected from or about them, including identifiers and factors specific to their identity. The processing of Personal Data, which includes activities like collection, storage, transfer, and use, is stringently regulated by frameworks such as the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), the Law Enforcement Directive (LED), and the Digital Markets Act (DMA). These regulations emphasize potential risks to individual rights and freedoms, mandating that any information capable of identifying an individual falls under their purview. Key principles guiding its use include lawfulness, fairness, transparency, purpose limitation, adequacy, necessity, proportionality, and data minimization. Data must be collected for specified, explicit, and legitimate purposes and limited to what is necessary, with its processing subject to specific rules and safeguards. Data subjects or end-users have rights over this information, including portability, objection, and the right not to be subject to solely automated decisions. The processing purposes and legal basis, such as explicit consent, for handling Personal Data, including salaries and medical information, must always be clear, and storage periods vary based on data categories and processing purposes, determined by the data controller. Special protection is afforded to children's Personal Data. Entities such as retailers, social media providers, advertisers, banking services, and "gatekeepers" (as defined by regulations) are subject to these regulations when processing Personal Data about employees, customers, or applicants, or data from public registries. This data is frequently used for creating profiles, defining targeting criteria, targeted and behavioral advertising, campaign reporting, training AI models, and automated decision-making. Such processing often requires a valid legal basis, and many digital services require this data to function, prompting explicit disclosure and authorization when an application collects it. Due to inherent privacy risks, Personal Data necessitates careful handling and may be subject to delisting requests. In connected vehicles, Personal Data collected through in-vehicle applications demands user control and privacy considerations and must be deleted upon vehicle ownership change if no longer needed. Personal Data is subject to stringent transfer requirements, especially when sent by an exporter to an importer in a third country or international organization, or when processed outside the EU, requiring appropriate safeguards. Any further use involves specific information and compatibility obligations under GDPR. Regulations mandate its protection through appropriate technical and organizational measures, privacy-enhancing technologies, and default settings to ensure the highest possible security level, safeguarding against unauthorized processing, loss, destruction, or damage. Its processing is also subject to transparency, storage limitations, and accessibility controls, often employing pseudonymization. It is encouraged to be stored in a structured, machine-readable format to support reuse and portability, with choices and explanations provided to the data subject. When shared by gatekeepers or undergoing processing by automated means, it requires anonymization, as outlined in GDPR Article 20 and DMA Article 6(9). Gatekeepers are responsible for protecting it against unauthorized disclosure and ensuring its secure transmission, requiring explicit consent for international transfers or sharing with third parties. Breaches involving Personal Data can lead to significant adverse effects on individuals, including loss of control, identity theft, financial loss, and damage to reputation. This information, which may be encrypted or exfiltrated during a breach, is subject to internal security handling and risk mitigation measures. Its exfiltration, sensitivity, volume, and context are factored into risk assessments and constitute a data breach, triggering notification obligations under regulations like GDPR, though if securely encrypted, notification to an authority may not be required. If disclosed to unauthorized parties, it can lead to a confidentiality breach and potential risk to individuals. Processing of Personal Data about natural persons, especially when on a large scale or involving regular and systematic monitoring, may lead to the need for Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) and can necessitate the designation of a Data Protection Officer (DPO), with processing operations determining the required level of DPO expertise. Personal data is often processed in a cross-border manner, making the identification of a lead supervisory authority relevant. Data subjects have the right to portability over this information, including data provided by them, inferred data, and derived data. Overall, Personal Data requires protection through legislation, investigations, and international cooperation, particularly concerning transfers to third countries and processing activities.
Under GDPR, Processing Already Under Way Processing already under way must be brought into conformity with this Regulation within two years of its entry into force. This Regulation.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Personal data can be processed for the purposes of planning and organisation of work. Planning and Organisation of Work.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Personal data can be processed for the purposes of recruitment. Recruitment Purposes.
Under GDPR, Education Research from registries identifies correlations with education. Long-Term Correlation of Social Conditions.
Under GDPR, Data Protection Supervisory Authorities Members of the supervisory authority are to be appointed by means of a transparent procedure. Transparent Procedure.
Under GDPR, Member State The Parliament in a Member State may appoint members of the supervisory authority. Parliament.
Under GDPR, Complainant The complainant makes a claim against the controller, alleging an infringement.<SEP>The controller and complainant interact regarding the cross-border processing. Controller.
Under GDPR, Data Protection Supervisory Authorities The lead authority cooperates with other supervisory authorities concerned to adopt binding decisions. Lead Authority.
Under GDPR, Controller Supervisory authorities from Member States where a controller has establishments have the right to participate in joint operations. Joint Operations.

Under GDPR, Consent Pursuant to Directive 95/46/EC Processing based on consent under Directive 95/46/EC does not require new consent if it aligns with this Regulation's conditions. Processing Already Under Way.
Under GDPR, Pseudonymisation The recipient of pseudonymised data must not be able to lift the technical and organisational measures implementing pseudonymisation. Recipient of Pseudonymised Data.
Under GDPR, Accreditation Criteria The Supervisory Authority submits draft accreditation criteria to the Board. Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Consistency Mechanism Standard contractual clauses are adopted in accordance with the consistency mechanism. Standard Contractual Clauses.
Under GDPR, Head of State The Head of State in a Member State may appoint members of the supervisory authority. Member State.

Under GDPR, Competent Data Protection Supervisory Authorities The European Commission coordinates, cooperates, and consults with competent data protection supervisory authorities. European Commission.
Under GDPR, Management Personal data can be processed for the purposes of management. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Official Authorities Official Authorities process Personal Data for aims of religious associations. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Independent Body An independent body in a Member State, under Member State law, may be entrusted with the appointment of supervisory authority members. Member State.

Under GDPR, Controller The controller should differentiate between access privileges of authorized personnel and limit access to data for employees.<SEP>Employees are generally not considered controllers but act under the authority of the controller.<SEP>An employee under the direct authority of the controller can process personal data. Employee.
Under GDPR, Board The Board adopts a decision within one month unless extended. One Month.
Under GDPR, Article 60 Article 60 is part of the GDPR and specifies cooperation procedures. GDPR.

Under GDPR, European Research Area The term "processing for the purpose of scientific research" should take into account the Union's objective of achieving a European Research Area under Article 179 (1) TFEU. Processing for the Purpose of Scientific Research.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Personal data can be processed for the purpose of the termination of the employment relationship. Termination of the Employment Relationship.
Under GDPR, EDPB The EDPB publishes the final national decision(s) in its register. Register.
Under GDPR, Direct Marketing This judgment provides legal precedent for the notion of direct marketing. StWL Städtische Werke Lauf a.d. Pegnitz v eprimo GmbH, C-102/20.
Under GDPR, Article 60 Procedure The Lead Supervisory Authority decides whether to handle a case in accordance with the Article 60 Procedure. Lead Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Commission National Authorities, including data protection supervisory authorities, are expected to cooperate and coordinate their actions with the Commission. National Authorities.
Under GDPR, European Research Area The European Union aims to achieve a European Research Area. The European Union.
Under GDPR, Employees' Personal Data Employees' personal data may be processed for the purpose of recruitment. Recruitment.
Under GDPR, Data Protection Training Appropriate data protection training is required for personnel having permanent or regular access to personal data. Personnel.

The Commission, when preparing and drawing-up delegated acts, should ensure a simultaneous, timely and appropriate transmission of relevant documents to the European Parliament and to the Council. (167) In order to ensure uniform conditions for the implementation of this Regulation, implementing powers should be conferred on the Commission when provided for by this Regulation. Those powers should be exercised in accordance with Regulation (EU) No 182/2011. In that context, the Commission should consider specific measures for micro, small and medium-sized enterprises. (168) The examination procedure should be used for the adoption of implementing acts on standard contractual clauses between controllers and processors and between processors; codes of conduct; technical standards and mechanisms for certification; the adequate level of protection afforded by a third country, a territory or a specified sector within that third country, or an international organisation; standard protection clauses; formats and procedures for the exchange of information by electronic means between controllers, processors and supervisory authorities for binding corporate rules; mutual assistance; and arrangements for the exchange of information by electronic means between supervisory authorities, and between supervisory authorities and the Board. (169) The Commission should adopt immediately applicable implementing acts where available evidence reveals that a third country, a territory or a specified sector within that third country, or an international organisation does not ensure an adequate level of protection, and imperative grounds of urgency so require. (170) Since the objective of this Regulation, namely to ensure an equivalent level of protection of natural persons and the free flow of personal data throughout the Union, cannot be sufficiently achieved by the Member States and can rather, by reason of the scale or effects of the action, be better achieved at Union level, the Union may adopt measures, in accordance with the principle of subsidiarity as set out in Article 5 of the Treaty on European Union (TEU). In accordance with the principle of proportionality as set out in that Article, this Regulation does not go beyond what is necessary in order to achieve that objective. (171) Directive 95/46/EC should be repealed by this Regulation. Processing already under way on the date of application of this Regulation should be brought into conformity with this Regulation within the period of two years after which this Regulation enters into force. Where processing is based on consent pursuant to Directive 95/46/EC, it is not necessary for the data subject to give his or her consent again if the manner in which the consent has been given is in line with the conditions of this Regulation, so as to allow the controller to continue such processing after the date of application of this Regulation. Commission decisions adopted and authorisations by supervisory authorities based on Directive 95/46/EC remain in force until amended, replaced or repealed. This Regulation lays down rules relating to the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data and rules relating to the free movement of personal data. 2. This Regulation protects fundamental rights and freedoms of natural persons and in particular their right to the protection of personal data. 3. The free movement of personal data within the Union shall be neither restricted nor prohibited for reasons connected with the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data. Article 2 Material scope 1. This Regulation applies to the processing of personal data wholly or partly by automated means and to the processing other than by automated means of personal data which form part of a filing system or are intended to form part of a filing system. 2. The shared data that constitutes personal data has to be anonymised. The Guidelines clarify the objective of Article 6(11) DMA to foster contestability in the online search engine market and how to achieve effective anonymisation of shared search data while taking into account such objectives. Article 7 DMA requires gatekeepers designated in relation to their number-independent interpersonal communications services to offer interoperability to alternative service providers requesting it. The Guidelines elaborate on the requirements of Article 7 that are relevant from the perspective of privacy and data protection law. Most notably, they recall that gatekeepers should comply with data minimisation and other GDPR principles when making their services interoperable, and mention necessary, proportionate, and justified measures that a gatekeeper may apply to ensure that third-party service providers requesting interoperability do not endanger the integrity, security and privacy of its services. The Guidelines also pronounce on coordination, cooperation and consultation between the European Commission as sole enforcer of the DMA and competent data protection supervisory authorities as enforcers of the GDPR. The Guidelines recall, with reference to relevant CJEU case law concerning the principle of sincere cooperation and ne bis in idem, that cooperation and coordination between the European Commission and data protection supervisory authorities is essential to ensure a consistent, effective and complementary application of the DMA and EU data protection law. ________________ Table of Contents 1 [Source: guidelines_202202_on_the_application_of_article_60_gdpr_en] "”. 88 The SAs should make sure that the application of such definitions is consistent with the understanding of the terms as set out in this section. Adopted 43 of the cause of action - that is to say the complainant obtained the vindication of his/her rights through the intervention of the LSA towards the controller, which meanwhile met the terms of the complainant’s claim. In such cases, providing that the complainant has been informed in the course of the procedure about the favourable result achieved, the LSA may decide to no longer take action in relation to the controller – i.e. none of the factors mentioned above in respect of dismissal/rejection vs. taking action is applicable. 231. This is the case, in particular, with the amicable settlement situation - i.e. the situation where the case has been resolved to a satisfaction of a data subject, when the infringement alleged in the complaint has been identified by the LSA and when the complainant agreed to an amicable resolution of this complaint. This situation falls within the remit of Article 60(7). Indeed, as already pointed out, the decision does not adversely affect the complainant, who manifested his or her satisfaction with the proposed settlement, and as such is not to be adopted by the complaint receiving SA under Article 60(8) or (9) – there being no dismissal or rejection at play. It will be for the LSA to adopt the final decision in such a case89, to take stock of the achieved settlement in its capacity as the sole interlocutor of the controller/processor under Article 56(6). 232. This also applies to cases that do not fall within the amicable settlement constellation, as the LSA did not or could not attempt such a settlement, but, nevertheless, its intervention during the handling of the complaint led the controller to stop the infringement and fully satisfy the complainant’s claim. In view of this result and of the specific circumstances of the case, the LSA may consider that the most adequate decision for the complaint at hand is to terminate the handling of the case, taking note of the achieved solution, and without taking any action towards the controller90. 233. However, since an infringement was indeed identified by the LSA, the decision not to take any action towards the controller would have to be based on the careful assessment of the circumstances of the complaint as a whole, in order to keep the same level of guarantees afforded to the data subjects. 234. On the other hand, within this context the final decision will not be issued by the complaint receiving SA but instead by the LSA, as per paragraph (7), even though no action is to be taken in relation to the controller through such final decision – in recognition of the LSA’s role as the sole interlocutor of the controller targeted by the complaint at issue and the fact that a finding of an infringement can have an adverse effect on the controller. This would render it impossible for the complainant to challenge the decision in the MS where the complaint was lodged, regardless of whether the complainant still has or has not cause of action to seek judicial remedy against a SA. That is a matter for the courts to determine in the concrete case. Therefore, whenever this scenario may happen, it should be ensured by the LSA via the complaint receiving SA that the complainant is duly informed on the positive achievement and on the envisaged outcome of the complaint and expresses no disagreement. 235. Lastly, it should be recalled that also the decision not to take action towards the controller, even when an infringement took place, has to have been agreed by the LSA and other CSAs, which entails that all 89 As this is the general rule, see also ECJ Case C‑645/19 para. 56 : ″In accordance with Article 60(7) of that regulation, it is the responsibility of the lead supervisory authority, as a general rule, to adopt a decision with respect to the cross-border processing concerned,…″ 90 This is without prejudice to the assessment on what is “the extent appropriate” to which the complaint is to be investigated pursuant to Article 57(1)(f), for which discretion lies with the SAs. Adopted 44 the circumstances of the case were duly taken into account, including the guarantee of the rights and freedoms of the complainant. Example 10: Unhappy customer submits a complaint to its local SA in MS A. The SA performs the preliminary vetting and forwards the complaint to the LSA. After receiving the complaint, and, upon investigating the issue, the LSA cannot find evidence to support the complaint. Therefore, the LSA is unable to determine the infringement and concludes that the complaint is to be rejected/dismissed, as no action is taken on the controller. The LSA shares a draft decision to that effect and as no objection is raised, pursuant to Article 60(8) the complaint receiving SA will adopt the final national decision rejecting/dismissing the complaint and notify it to the complainant. Example 11: A complainant indicates he sent a request to a controller and did not receive any answer. The DPA does not receive any response to its preliminary vetting actions. The complaint receiving SA sends the case to the LSA. The LSA notes that the request was sent to a wrong/non-existent address by comparison to what is indicated on the website of the controller (the complainant seems to have tried a contact@XXX.com address without checking the contact addresses mentioned on the website).
