[Source: joint_com-edpb_gls_interplay_dma_gdpr_for_public_consultation_en] "to provide effective interoperability. Any such collection and exchange of the personal data of end users shall fully comply with Regulation (EU) 2016/679 and Directive 2002/58/EC.” Lastly, Article 7(9) DMA provides that “The gatekeeper shall not be prevented from taking measures to ensure that third-party providers of NIICS 154 For the definition of NIICS, see Article 2(9) DMA, referring to the definition contained in Article 2(7) of Directive (EU) 2018/1972. 155 Recital 64. 156 According to Article 2(29) DMA, “‘interoperability’ means the ability to exchange information and mutually use the information which has been exchanged through interfaces or other solutions, so that all elements of hardware or software work with other hardware and software and with users in all the ways in which they are intended to function.” 157 Article 7(4) DMA. 158 Recital 64 DMA. requesting interoperability do not endanger the integrity, security and privacy of its services, provided that such measures are strictly necessary and proportionate and are duly justified.” 195. To establish interoperability, the duties and responsibilities of two parties – i.e., the gatekeeper and the NIICS provider requesting interoperability – should be considered. 196. On the side of the NIICS provider requesting interoperability, the provider will need to comply and implement the technical details and general terms and conditions for interoperability set out by the reference offer of the gatekeeper, including in what concerns personal data processing that is necessary to enable interoperability. From the moment in which interoperability is established, the NIICS provider requesting interoperability is responsible for complying with Union data protection law in relation to the personal data it accesses (e.g., when providing the NIICS to the end user that decides to make use of the interoperable basic functionalities). 197. Pursuant to Article 7(4) DMA, the gatekeeper is required to draft and publish a reference offer with technical details, general terms and conditions on interoperability. The Reference Offer should ensure effective interoperability with the basic functionalities listed in Article 7(2) DMA. Such reference offer and more broadly the gatekeeper’s measures to ensure compliance with Article 7 DMA should ensure full compliance with Union data protection law,159 including that only personal data that is strictly necessary for provision of effective interoperability is processed in compliance with Article 5(1), point (c) GDPR. 198. The implementation of interoperability by the gatekeeper under Article 7 DMA is very likely to fulfil the criteria for the requirement to carry out a data protection impact assessment under Article 35 GDPR160 which includes an assessment of possible risks to the rights and freedoms of data subjects arising from data exchange that is needed for the purpose of ensuring interoperability, as well as appropriate, timely and effective measures to tackle the risks identified. 199. Under Article 7(8) DMA, the gatekeeper has to ensure full compliance with Union data protection legislation when sharing personal data with providers of NIICS requesting interoperability. In particular, the gatekeeper is obliged to collect and share only personal data that is strictly necessary to provide effective interoperability and so in line with GDPR and the ePrivacy Directive. In line with Article 5(1), point (c) GDPR, controllers should consider if the purpose of the processing requires use of personal data or if anonymised data can be used and using the latter where possible. This data minimisation principle is particularly relevant in the case where gatekeepers need to collect additional personal data from end users that the gatekeeper does not use itself when providing its own communication service, for the sole purpose of providing effective interoperability, while meeting all requirements of Article 7 DMA and Union data protection law, in particular to maintain the level of security across interoperable services. 159 Article 8(1) DMA. 160 See Article 29 Data Protection Working Party Guidelines on Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) and determining whether processing is “likely to result in a high risk” for the purposes of Regulation 2016/679 (WP 248 rev.01), As last Revised and Adopted on 4 October 2017, p. 10. Interoperability under article 7 DMA is likely to satisfy more than two of the criteria mentioned, i.e. it relates to data of a highly personal nature (electronic communications whose confidentiality should be protected), is large scale, and relies on innovative technical processes. 200. The other parts of this section look at the interplay between Union data protection law and the DMA, in relation to the categories of personal data that are necessary for the provision of interoperability (cf. section 7.1), personal data processing to preserve the level of security across interoperable services (section 7.2) and other situations where personal data processing may take place in the context of Article 7 interoperability (section 7.3). 7.1 Categories of personal data necessary to ensure interoperability 201. Personal data needed to ensure interoperability can be separated in the following three major categories: a. the message content themselves that would be exchanged between servers of the gatekeeper and those of the other providers. b. the personal data that is needed to ensure technical aspects of interoperability between the NIICS of the gatekeeper and of the provider requesting interoperability, including to ensure that end users of the different NIICS can address each other and communicate."

[Source: edpb_guidelines_202401_legitimateinterest_en] "ed on 9 April 2014), p. 35. 123 CJEU, judgment of 4 July 2023, Case C-252/21, Meta v. Bundeskartellamt (ECLI:EU:C:2023:537), para. 109. 124 See further the section 7.1 on “transmission to competent authorities” below in these guidelines. 125 For more details, see para. 26 of the present guidelines. 30 Adopted - version for public consultation 109. According to Recital 47 GDPR, the processing of personal data for direct marketing purposes may be regarded as carried out for a legitimate interest. Direct marketing is not defined in the GDPR. However, CJEU case law suggests that personalised advertising could be considered a form of direct marketing.126 Moreover, the CJEU has interpreted the notion of communication for direct marketing purposes under the ePrivacy Directive,127 which is closely linked to the GDPR128 and regulates the sending of direct marketing communications.129 In particular, the CJEU found that to assess whether a communication is made for direct marketing purposes it must be ascertained whether such a communication pursues a commercial purpose and is addressed directly and individually to a consumer.130 In this respect the CJEU found that it is irrelevant whether the advertising at issue is addressed to a predetermined and individually identified recipient or is sent on a mass, random basis to multiple recipients.131 What matters is that there is a communication for a commercial purpose, which reaches, directly and individually, a consumer.132 In this regard, it should be noted that the Court found, for instance, that, based on these criteria, advertising consisting in displaying advertising banners, disguised as emails, into the private email inboxes of the users of an email service – funded by advertising and provided for free to users – is a form of direct marketing (even though such advertising does not entail the sending of an email to a specific consumer).133 This understanding of the notion of direct marketing purposes may in principle be used by way of analogy to understand its meaning also within the GDPR. This is because the interpretation of a provision of EU law requires that account be taken not only of its wording and the objectives it pursues, but also of its context and the provisions of EU law as a whole. 134 110. The fact that Recital 47 GDPR states that the processing of personal data for direct marketing purposes may be carried out to fulfil a legitimate interest does not mean that direct marketing always constitutes a legitimate interest, and that it is automatically possible to rely on Article 6(1)(f) GDPR to engage in all kinds of direct marketing activities. 111. For some cases of direct marketing, a different legal basis – such as consent – may be required, thus precluding the use of legitimate interest as a legal basis in this context, as explained below. 112. Furthermore, it has to be borne in mind that, as outlined above, reliance on legitimate interest requires that three cumulative conditions be fulfilled, namely, first, the pursuit of a legitimate interest by the controller or by a third party; second, the need to process personal data for the purposes of the legitimate interests pursued; and third, that the interests or fundamental freedoms and rights of the person concerned by the relevant processing do not take precedence over the legitimate interest of the controller or of a third party (see further Chapter II above in these guidelines). 135 It follows from this that the processing of personal data for direct marketing purposes cannot be based on Article 6(1)(f) GDPR if these 126 CJEU, judgment of 4 July 2023, Case C-252/21, Meta v. Bundeskartellamt (ECLI:EU:C:2023:537), para. 115. 127 CJEU, judgment of 25 November 2021, Case C‑102/20, StWL Städtische Werke Lauf a.d. Pegnitz (ECLI:EU:C:2021:954), paras. 47-50. 128 The ePrivacy Directive seeks to translate the principles set out in the data protection legal framework into specific rules for the telecommunications sector (see Recital 4 ePrivacy Directive). See further EDPB, Opinion 5/2019 on the interplay between the ePrivacy Directive and the GDPR, in particular regarding the competence, tasks and powers of data protection authorities, adopted on 12 March 2019. 129 See Article 13 ePrivacy Directive. 130 CJEU, judgment of 25 November 2021, Case C‑102/20, StWL Städtische Werke Lauf a.d. Pegnitz (ECLI:EU:C:2021:954), para. 47. 131 Ibid, para. 50. 132 Ibid. 133 Ibid., paras. 19-22 and 47-51. 134 CJEU, judgment of 10 December 2018, Case C-621/18, Wightman and Others (ECLI:EU:C:2018:999), para. 47. For an example of how a term in an EU legal act can be interpreted by looking at how the same term is used in other EU acts, see Opinion of Advocate General Tanchev in Case C-617/15, Hummel Holding (ECLI:EU:C:2017:13), paras. 30 and following. 135 CJEU, judgment of 4 July 2023, Case C-252/21, Meta v. Bundeskartellamt (ECLI:EU"

## Test Case 63 - Warehouse Optimization - Amount of Correct Activities: 5 - Amount of False Positives: 3 - Amount of False Negatives: 0 - **Expected:** send to customer (sid-B605E9E8-4D14-48B5-AE6C-B62AE7B335E4), capture order (sid-1D1EF4B0-0651-4909-9B4D-0C8C61F4758E), analyze the customers (sid-70874A34-B162-4AB5-8AC0-DE8059C26FDB), forward order (sid-5100EBA3-E14F-44FE-AF0A-3C8304FD35B6), make offer and sell (sid-EB25994B-55A9-40B3-958B-DB47E77C8728) - **Actual:** capture order (sid-1D1EF4B0-0651-4909-9B4D-0C8C61F4758E), forward order (sid-5100EBA3-E14F-44FE-AF0A-3C8304FD35B6), make offer and sell (sid-EB25994B-55A9-40B3-958B-DB47E77C8728), send to customer (sid-B605E9E8-4D14-48B5-AE6C-B62AE7B335E4), analyze the customers (sid-70874A34-B162-4AB5-8AC0-DE8059C26FDB), determine sales and inventory (sid-AD6B4C0B-F71E-4A64-B751-485EFB5BDB74), determine result of market research (sid-7FE2BAAA-9BB8-4791-8BA5-08ED0864351B), prepare optimization report (sid-B9F0696C-3718-40CE-BDEE-006D25D213CC) - **Result:** ❌ Failed - **False Positives:** determine sales and inventory (sid-AD6B4C0B-F71E-4A64-B751-485EFB5BDB74), determine result of market research (sid-7FE2BAAA-9BB8-4791-8BA5-08ED0864351B), prepare optimization report (sid-B9F0696C-3718-40CE-BDEE-006D25D213CC) - **False Negatives:** None - **Amount of Retries:** 0 ### RAG Metrics (Ragas) - **Faithfulness:** 0.042 - **Context Utilization:** 0.189 - **Samples:** 8 (failed: 0)

Reasoning of the LLM

 - **capture order (sid-1D1EF4B0-0651-4909-9B4D-0C8C61F4758E)**: The capture order task collects customer order details, which include personal identifiers such as name, address, and contact information. This is a direct collection of personal data. - **forward order (sid-5100EBA3-E14F-44FE-AF0A-3C8304FD35B6)**: The forward order task forwards the order information to the next department. The order contains personal data of the customer, so the task processes personal data. - **make offer and sell (sid-EB25994B-55A9-40B3-958B-DB47E77C8728)**: The make offer and sell task creates an offer and a sales record that includes the customer’s personal data, such as contact details and payment information. - **send to customer (sid-B605E9E8-4D14-48B5-AE6C-B62AE7B335E4)**: The send to customer task transmits order confirmation or other communications to the customer, using the customer’s personal data (e.g., email address). This is a transmission of personal data. - ** analyze the customers (sid-70874A34-B162-4AB5-8AC0-DE8059C26FDB)**: The analyze the customers task processes customer data to derive insights. The data used contains personal identifiers, so the task processes personal data. - **determine sales and inventory (sid-AD6B4C0B-F71E-4A64-B751-485EFB5BDB74)**: The determine sales and inventory task reads sales records from the database, which include customer personal data. Therefore it processes personal data. - **determine result of market research (sid-7FE2BAAA-9BB8-4791-8BA5-08ED0864351B)**: The determine result of market research task uses market research data that may contain personal data of respondents. Thus it processes personal data. - **prepare optimization report (sid-B9F0696C-3718-40CE-BDEE-006D25D213CC)**: The prepare optimization report task reads from the database, which likely contains sales and customer data. Consequently, it processes personal data.

result.reason: The task "Zulassungs-kriterien automatisiert prüfen" collects and evaluates application data such as student identifiers, contact details, and eligibility criteria, which are personal data. It processes this information to determine admission eligibility.

The task "Ranking automatisiert durchführen" performs automated ranking of applicants based on personal data such as academic records, application details, and eligibility criteria. This involves processing personal data to produce a ranking outcome.

The task "Wartelistenplatz(SPZ)/Absage zuweisen/erteilen" assigns waiting list positions or rejects applicants, which requires processing personal data such as applicant identifiers, contact information, and application status.

The task "Zulassung (SPZ)/Zulassung zuweisen/erteilen" grants admission to applicants, which involves processing personal data such as applicant identifiers, contact details, and eligibility information.

ragPromptContext:
[concept] Accreditation Criteria — The standards and conditions a body must meet to be accredited to monitor codes of conduct.
Criteria for accreditation of bodies, approval of which requires an opinion from the Board as per Article 41(3) or Article 43(3).
[person] Auditor — An auditor, who can be mandated by the controller, conducts inspections/audits to verify compliance.

[organization] Lead Supervisory Authority — The Lead Supervisory Authority (LSA) is a central concept within data protection under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), specifically designed for cross-border processing activities. Also recognized as the "one-stop-shop" mechanism, the LSA is the supervisory authority situated in the Member State of a controller's or processor's main establishment. Its primary role is to lead GDPR enforcement for cases that span multiple Member States, acting as the sole point of contact for the controller or processor in such multi-jurisdictional data protection scenarios. The LSA has a leading function in cooperation procedures, explicitly mandated to collaborate with other Concerned Supervisory Authorities (CSAs). Its responsibilities include overseeing data protection operations, conducting investigations, and sharing relevant information with other CSAs. The LSA is also tasked with preparing and submitting draft decisions under Articles 60 and 62, striving for consensus. It plays a crucial role in the Article 60 procedure. Furthermore, the LSA is responsible for handling objections raised by other CSAs. Should a dispute arise where the LSA does not follow a relevant and reasoned objection, or if it deems an objection not relevant or reasoned, it refers the matter to the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) for resolution. The LSA also conducts joint operations, facilitates information exchange, and serves as the primary authority a controller must notify in the event of a cross-border processing breach. As specified in GDPR Article 56, the LSA is a specific supervisory authority for transnational code members, underscoring its integral role in managing cross-border data protection and ensuring compliance, including taking measures intended to produce legal effects like administrative fines.
The Lead Supervisory Authority is the primary interlocutor for the Commission in cases where a gatekeeper has a "main establishment" under the GDPR, responsible for cooperating with concerned supervisory authorities.

[concept] Authorisation and Advisory Powers — Authorisation and Advisory Powers are the capabilities of a Supervisory Authority to grant approvals, issue opinions, and provide guidance regarding data processing, including advising on prior consultation, issuing opinions to various bodies, authorizing processing, approving codes of conduct, accrediting certification bodies, issuing certifications, adopting standard clauses, authorizing contractual clauses and administrative arrangements, and approving binding corporate rules.

[location] Central Administration — A criterion used for identifying a controller's main establishment, specifically when it is not the place of the central administration in the EU.
Central administration refers to the primary place of a controller's or processor's management within the EU.
The place in the Union where a controller's main establishment is located, unless decisions on personal data processing are made elsewhere.

[method] Automated Processing — Automated processing is a form of data processing that does not require human intervention and is a key component of profiling.
Processing of data performed automatically, the basis for automated decision-making.
Processing of data without human involvement in the decision-making process.
Automated processing involves the use of technology to systematically and extensively evaluate personal aspects relating to natural persons.
Processing carried out without human intervention, which can produce legal effects concerning a data subject, such as automatic refusal of credit applications.
Automated Processing, including profiling, is a type of processing that may require a Data Protection Impact Assessment, especially if it involves systematic evaluation of personal aspects and produces legal effects.
Processing of personal data where decisions are made without human intervention, including profiling.

[concept] Core Platform Services (CPSs) — Core Platform Services are services to which the Digital Markets Act (DMA) applies, provided by undertakings designated as gatekeepers, and include categories such as online intermediation services, online search engines, and cloud computing services.
Core Platform Services (CPSs) are services provided by gatekeepers where vast amounts of personal data are collected.
Core Platform Services are relevant services for which data is provided or generated, falling under the data access obligation of gatekeepers.

[organization] Supervisory Authority — A Supervisory Authority (SA) is an independent public authority established by a European Union Member State, as mandated by Article 51 of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). Its core responsibility is to monitor and enforce the application of data protection law, primarily the GDPR, within its specific national jurisdiction. The overarching goal of an SA is to safeguard the fundamental rights and freedoms of individuals regarding the processing of their personal data and to facilitate the free flow of personal data within the European Union. SAs act as competent national bodies under Articles 55 or 56 of the GDPR, endowed with a broad spectrum of powers, including investigation, corrective actions, authorization, and advisory functions. Their decisions, which carry legal effects, are subject to judicial remedies, and they are obligated to respect the right to be heard. Supervisory Authorities are responsible for handling complaints lodged by data subjects, especially when controllers fail to address their requests. They are also the competent national authority to which personal data breaches must be notified without undue delay, typically within 72 hours unless the breach is unlikely to pose a risk to individuals. For cross-border breaches, an SA can act as the lead authority, request further details, advise on informing individuals, and order controllers to do so. They also receive information about the Data Protection Officer's (DPO) name and contact details. Supervisory Authorities are empowered to determine the nature and gravity of infringements, ensure compliance, and order appropriate measures against data controllers or processors. This includes the ability to impose effective, proportionate, and dissuasive administrative fines for GDPR infringements, considering factors such as circumstances, economic viability, and proportionality. They may hold parent companies liable and impose fines for non-compliance with Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) requirements. Additionally, a supervisory authority must be consulted if a DPIA indicates a high residual risk that cannot be mitigated, and it possesses the power to prohibit such processing operations. They can also require documentation of a DPO designation analysis and request access to records of processing operations. Beyond enforcement, Supervisory Authorities play a crucial role in GDPR accreditation and certification mechanisms. This involves approving criteria, reviewing existing certifications, accrediting certification bodies, and potentially acting as a certification body themselves. They can establish additional accreditation requirements, sometimes in cooperation with a National Accreditation Body. SAs are authorized to oversee and approve administrative arrangements for data transfers under Article 46(3)(b) GDPR, adopt standard contractual clauses for data processing matters, approve safeguards, and apply consistency mechanisms. They are also responsible for publishing lists of processing operations subject to mandatory DPIAs and for conducting prior consultations if DPIA results indicate a high risk or if existing operations have been pre-checked. While typically one lead supervisory authority exists for cross-border processing, some Member States may have more than one SA, all of whom are bound by the content of a draft decision once consensus is reached. They are also responsible for verifying compliance with data protection regulations and may require a controller to communicate a personal data breach to the data subject.
[organization] HLG — The HLG (High-Level Group) is an organization set up by a Commission Decision to provide advice and expertise to the Commission in areas falling within its members' competences.
[organization] Services Competent for Social Security Matters — Services Competent for Social Security Matters are entities responsible for social security, involved in international data exchange.

Under GDPR, Automated Decision-Making The controller must inform about the use of data for automated decision-making where relevant.<SEP>Controllers are subject to Article 22 provisions if they use a profile in a solely automated decision-making process with significant effects.<SEP>The controller uses and regularly tests automated decision-making methods. Controller.

Under GDPR, Controller The relationship between a Controller and a Lead Supervisory Authority is central to the oversight of cross-border data processing, particularly under regulations such as the GDPR. The Lead Supervisory Authority is identified for a Controller primarily based on its central administration or the location where decisions concerning processing are made. In situations involving both a Controller and a Processor, the Lead Supervisory Authority for the Controller typically assumes the role for both, as outlined in Recital 36. Furthermore, the supervisory authority of a Member State where a Controller's main establishment is located often acts as the competent Lead Supervisory Authority. This authority is competent to supervise cross-border processing activities conducted by a Controller. A key responsibility of the Controller in this relationship is to notify the Lead Supervisory Authority in the event of breaches involving cross-border processing. Beyond notification, the Lead Supervisory Authority serves as the sole interlocutor for the Controller regarding cross-border processing, meaning the Controller interacts exclusively with this authority. The Lead Supervisory Authority is responsible for adopting and notifying decisions to the Controller, who, in turn, is obligated to take measures to ensure compliance with these decisions. Lead Supervisory Authority.

Under GDPR, Controller The relationship between a Controller and a Supervisory Authority is established primarily by data protection regulations, such as the GDPR, and focuses on compliance, breach management, and accountability. A central obligation of the Controller is to notify the Supervisory Authority of personal data breaches, typically within 72 hours of becoming aware of the breach, especially if it poses a risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals, as per Article 33(1) and Article 27(5) GDPR. The Controller is responsible for determining if a breach is notifiable and must ensure the relevant Supervisory Authority is informed, potentially proactively notifying them of infringements or seeking advice. They must also inform the Supervisory Authority about data transfers, documenting assessments and safeguards. Controllers without an EU establishment are required to engage with local supervisory authorities in every Member State where they operate. Furthermore, the Controller must consult the Supervisory Authority if a high risk identified in a Data Protection Impact Assessment cannot be mitigated by reasonable means before processing. Controllers are also obligated to cooperate with and make records available to the Supervisory Authority upon request, demonstrating accountability and compliance. The Supervisory Authority plays a significant role in overseeing the Controller's data protection practices. It can advise Controllers on how to inform individuals about breaches and may even order them to do so, particularly for high-risk breaches. The Supervisory Authority verifies the Controller's compliance, handles complaints, and seeks amicable settlements regarding possible infringements. It possesses powers to require the Controller to provide information, carry out investigations, notify infringements, and obtain access to data and premises. The Supervisory Authority can issue warnings and reprimands, order compliance, and impose sanctions, including administrative fines under Article 83 GDPR, especially in cases of unjustified breaches. The level of the Controller's cooperation is considered by the Supervisory Authority when assessing mitigation efforts. The Supervisory Authority also promotes awareness among Controllers regarding their obligations under pertinent regulations and informs Controllers when a received complaint is dismissed or rejected. Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Accreditation Criteria The Supervisory Authority submits draft accreditation criteria to the Board. Supervisory Authority.

Under GDPR, Accreditation Criteria A supervisory authority aims to approve the criteria for accreditation of a body pursuant to Article 41(3) or a certification body pursuant to Article 43(3). Supervisory Authorities.

Under GDPR, Automated Processing Profiling is a form of automated processing, requiring it to be automated.<SEP>Automated processing can include profiling.<SEP>Automated processing, including profiling, evaluates personal aspects.<SEP>Profiling is a form of automated processing of personal data. Profiling.
Under GDPR, Core Platform Services (CPSs) CPSs are listed in a designation decision addressed to a gatekeeper by the Commission. Gatekeeper.

Under GDPR, Automated Decision-Making The GDPR specifically addresses automated decision-making, including provisions to protect individuals' rights.<SEP>The GDPR includes provisions that reference automated decision-making. GDPR.

Under GDPR, GDPR The Lead Supervisory Authority leads the enforcement of the GDPR for cross-border processing activities.<SEP>The GDPR provides for shared responsibility in monitoring its application, influencing the LSA's position. Lead Supervisory Authority.

Under GDPR, GDPR The Supervisory Authority determines its approach to certification to pursue the intent of GDPR, considering its tasks and powers under GDPR articles, and monitors and enforces the application of the regulation.<SEP>The supervisory authority's accreditation function is a task pursuant to GDPR Articles 57(1)(q) and 58(3)(e).<SEP>The GDPR empowers the Supervisory Authority to perform accreditation and certification functions.<SEP>Supervisory Authorities are tasked with enforcing the GDPR.<SEP>The supervisory authority oversees the application of the GDPR and receives DPIA reports under specific conditions. Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Online Search Engines Online search engines provide access to ranking, query, click, and view data. Ranking, Query, Click and View Data.
Under GDPR, Alphabet Alphabet was designated as a gatekeeper by Commission Decision C(2023) 6101 final. Commission Decisions C(2023) 6101 final.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller must provide a way for human intervention in automated decisions. Human Intervention.
Under GDPR, Controller The record of processing activities for a controller may include details of a joint controller. Joint Controller.
Under GDPR, Controller Supervisory authorities oversee the processing activities of controllers. Data Protection Supervisory Authorities.

Under GDPR, Consent The controller must inform data subjects about processing purposes to ensure valid consent.<SEP>Controllers must adhere to specific requirements to ensure consent is valid under the GDPR.<SEP>Controllers must obtain specific, granular, and informed consent from data subjects.<SEP>A controller must obtain and manage consent for processing personal data in accordance with GDPR.<SEP>If a controller relies on consent for processing, they must respect an individual's choice to withdraw it.<SEP>Controllers are responsible for obtaining and demonstrating valid consent.<SEP>A controller needs to demonstrate that it is possible to refuse or withdraw consent without detriment and ensure all conditions for consent are met. Controller.
Under GDPR, Certification Bodies Certification bodies must undertake to respect the criteria approved by the supervisory authority or the Board. Criteria.

Under GDPR, Administrative Arrangements Administrative arrangements require authorization from a competent supervisory authority.<SEP>The Supervisory Authority can authorize administrative arrangements. Supervisory Authority.

Under GDPR, Personal Data The shared data that constitutes personal data has to be anonymized under Article 6(11) DMA.<SEP>Ranking, query, click, and view data can constitute personal data, requiring anonymization. Ranking, Query, Click and View Data.
Under GDPR, Article 21(1) The Data Subject objects to processing pursuant to Article 21(1). Data Subject.
Under GDPR, Central Administration The main establishment of a controller in the Union should be its central administration in the Union. Controller.
Under GDPR, Controller Supervisory authorities from Member States where a controller has establishments have the right to participate in joint operations. Joint Operations.

Under GDPR, Consent Consent, in the context of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), is a fundamental concept for lawful processing of personal data. The GDPR defines consent and establishes it as one of the six lawful bases for processing personal data, explicitly listed in Article 6. It also sets forth specific conditions and requirements for valid consent, notably in Article 7, with Article 4(11) providing a crucial definitional baseline. The legal framework of the GDPR governs the validity of consent, playing a significant role in assessing whether consent is freely given, particularly as outlined in Article 7(4) and Recital 43. This framework includes stricter standards for how consent must be obtained and managed, requiring controllers to ensure their consent mechanisms are fully compliant with GDPR provisions. For specific types of sensitive data, such as biometric data, processing requires consent that adheres to the stricter requirements of Article 9(2)(a) GDPR. Furthermore, the notion of consent within the ePrivacy Directive is interpreted in light of the GDPR, aligning with the consent requirements provided by Articles 4(11) and 7 of the GDPR. This emphasizes that for data processing activities, particularly those involving cookies and similar technologies under the ePrivacy Directive, GDPR guidelines for consent must be followed to ensure legality and adherence to an internationally recognized privacy standard.<SEP>Consent is defined and regulated under the GDPR as one of the legal grounds for processing personal data.<SEP>The GDPR provides the legal definition of "consent" for data subjects. GDPR.
Under GDPR, Commission The Commission assesses the existence and effective functioning of Independent Supervisory Authorities when determining adequacy. Independent Supervisory Authorities.
Under GDPR, Code of Conduct The Supervisory Authority provides an opinion on, approves, registers, and publishes codes of conduct. Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Automated Individual Decision-Making Automated individual decision-making includes profiling. Profiling.
Under GDPR, Controller or Processor The supervisory authority notifies its final decision to the controller or the processor. Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Automated Individual Decision-Making The GDPR contains general provisions that apply to automated individual decision-making. GDPR.

Under GDPR, Controller The controller provides contact details for the controller's representative.<SEP>The record of processing activities for a controller may include details of the controller's representative. Controller's Representative.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Confidentiality breach of personal data that were encrypted with a state of the art algorithm still needs to be notified. Supervisory Authority.

[Source: General_Data_Protection_Regulation_en] "of this Article shall contain mechanisms which enable the body referred to in Article 41(1) to carry out the mandatory monitoring of compliance with its provisions by the controllers or processors which undertake to apply it, without prejudice to the tasks and powers of supervisory authorities competent pursuant to Article 55 or 56. 5. Associations and other bodies referred to in paragraph 2 of this Article which intend to prepare a code of conduct or to amend or extend an existing code shall submit the draft code, amendment or extension to the supervisory authority which is competent pursuant to Article 55. The supervisory authority shall provide an opinion on whether the draft code, amendment or extension complies with this Regulation and shall approve that draft code, amendment or extension if it finds that it provides sufficient appropriate safeguards. 6. Where the draft code, or amendment or extension is approved in accordance with paragraph 5, and where the code of conduct concerned does not relate to processing activities in several Member States, the supervisory authority shall register and publish the code. 7. Where a draft code of conduct relates to processing activities in several Member States, the supervisory authority which is competent pursuant to Article 55 shall, before approving the draft code, amendment or extension, submit it in the procedure referred to in Article 63 to the Board which shall provide an opinion on whether the draft code, amendment or extension complies with this Regulation or, in the situation referred to in paragraph 3 of this Article, provides appropriate safeguards. 8. Where the opinion referred to in paragraph 7 confirms that the draft code, amendment or extension complies with this Regulation, or, in the situation referred to in paragraph 3, provides appropriate safeguards, the Board shall submit its opinion to the Commission. 9. The Commission may, by way of implementing acts, decide that the approved code of conduct, amendment or extension submitted to it pursuant to paragraph 8 of this Article have general validity within the Union. Those implementing acts shall be adopted in accordance with the examination procedure set out in Article 93(2). 4.5.2016 L 119/57 Official Journal of the European Union EN 10. The Commission shall ensure appropriate publicity for the approved codes which have been decided as having general validity in accordance with paragraph 9. 11. The Board shall collate all approved codes of conduct, amendments and extensions in a register and shall make them publicly available by way of appropriate means. Article 41 Monitoring of approved codes of conduct 1. Without prejudice to the tasks and powers of the competent supervisory authority under Articles 57 and 58, the monitoring of compliance with a code of conduct pursuant to Article 40 may be carried out by a body which has an appropriate level of expertise in relation to the subject-matter of the code and is accredited for that purpose by the competent supervisory authority. 2. A body as referred to in paragraph 1 may be accredited to monitor compliance with a code of conduct where that body has: (a) demonstrated its independence and expertise in relation to the subject-matter of the code to the satisfaction of the competent supervisory authority; (b) established procedures which allow it to assess the eligibility of controllers and processors concerned to apply the code, to monitor their compliance with its provisions and to periodically review its operation; (c) established procedures and structures to handle complaints about infringements of the code or the manner in which the code has been, or is being, implemented by a controller or processor, and to make those procedures and structures transparent to data subjects and the public; and (d) demonstrated to the satisfaction of the competent supervisory authority that its tasks and duties do not result in a conflict of interests. 3. The competent supervisory authority shall submit the draft criteria for accreditation of a body as referred to in paragraph 1 of this Article to the Board pursuant to the consistency mechanism referred to in Article 63. 4. Without prejudice to the tasks and powers of the competent supervisory authority and the provisions of Chapter VIII, a body as referred to in paragraph 1 of this Article shall, subject to appropriate safeguards, take appropriate action in cases of infringement of the code by a controller or processor, including suspension or exclusion of the controller or processor concerned from the code. It shall inform the competent supervisory authority of such actions and the reasons for taking them. 5. The competent supervisory authority shall revoke the accreditation of a body as referred to in paragraph 1 if the conditions for accreditation are not, or are no longer, met or where actions taken by the body infringe this Regulation. 6. This Article shall not apply to processing carried out by public authorities and bodies. Article 42 Certification 1. The Member States, the supervisory authorities, the Board and the Commission shall encourage, in particular at Union level, the establishment of data protection certification mechanisms and of data protection seals and marks, for the purpose of demonstrating compliance with this Regulation of processing operations by controllers and processors. The specific needs of micro, small and medium-sized enterprises shall be taken into account. 4.5.2016 L 119/58 Official Journal of the European Union EN 2. In addition to adherence by controllers or processors subject to this Regulation, data protection certification mechanisms, seals or marks approved pursuant to paragraph 5 of this Article may be established for the purpose of demonstrating the existence of appropriate safeguards provided by controllers or processors that are not subject to this Regulation pursuant to Article 3 within the framework of personal data transfers to third countries or international organisations under the terms referred to in point (f) of Article 46(2)."

Identifying a lead supervisory authority: the key concepts. 1.1 ‘Cross-border processing of personal data’. Identifying a lead supervisory authority is only relevant where a controller or processor is carrying out the cross-border processing of personal data. Article 4(23) of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) defines ‘cross-border processing’ as either the: - processing of personal data which takes place in the context of the activities of establishments in more than one Member State of a controller or processor in the Union where the controller or processor is established in more than one Member State; or the - processing of personal data which takes place in the context of the activities of a single establishment of a controller or processor in the Union but which substantially affects or is likely to substantially affect data subjects in more than one Member State. This means that where an organisation has establishments in France and Romania, for example, and the processing of personal data takes place in the context of their activities, then this will constitute cross-border processing. Alternatively, the organisation may only carry out processing activity in the context of its establishment in France. However, if the activity substantially affects – or is likely to substantially affect - data subjects in France and Romania then this will also constitute cross- border processing. 1.1.1 ‘Substantially affects’. The GDPR does not define ‘substantially’ or ‘affects’. The intention of the wording was to ensure that not all processing activity, with any effect and that takes place within the context of a single establishment, falls within the definition of ‘cross-border processing’. The most relevant ordinary English meanings of ‘substantial’ include; ‘of ample or considerable amount or size; sizeable, fairly large’, or ‘having solid worth or value, of real significance; solid; weighty, important’ (Oxford English Dictionary). The most relevant meaning of the verb ‘affect’ is ‘to influence’ or ‘to make a material impression on’. The related noun -‘effect’- means, amongst other things, ‘a result’ or ‘a consequence’ (Oxford English Dictionary). This suggests that for data processing to affect someone it must have some form of impact on them. Processing that does not have a substantial effect on individuals does not fall within the second part of the definition of ‘cross-border processing’. However, it would fall within the first part of the definition where the processing of personal data takes place in the context of the activities of establishments in more than one Member State of a controller or processor in the Union, where the controller or processor is established in more than one Member State. Processing can be brought within the second part of the definition if there is the likelihood of a substantial effect, not just an actual substantial effect. Note that ‘likely to’ does not mean that there is a remote possibility of a substantial effect. The substantial effect must be more likely than not. On the other hand, it also means that individuals do not have to be actually 4 affected: the likelihood of a substantial effect is sufficient to bring the processing within the definition of ‘cross-border processing’. The fact that a data processing operation may involve the processing of"

This may include, for example:  the information provided by the data subject on the application form;  information about previous account conduct , including any payment arrears; and  official public records information such as fraud record information and insolvency records. The controller also includes information to advise the data subject that the credit scoring methods used are regularly tested to ensure they remain fair, effective and unbiased. The controller provides contact details for the data subject to request that any declined decision is reconsidered, in line with the provisions of Article 22(3). ‘Significance’ and ‘envisaged consequences’ This term suggests that information must be provided about intended or future processing, and how the automated decision-making might affect the data subject.41 In order to make this information meaningful and understandable, real, tangible examples of the type of possible effects should be given. In a digital context, controllers might be able to use additional tools to help illustrate such effects. Example An insurance company uses an automated decision making process to set motor insurance premiums based on monitoring customers’ driving behaviour. To illustrate the significance and envisaged consequences of the processing it explains that dangerous driving may result in higher insurance payments and provides an app comparing fictional drivers, including one with dangerous driving habits such as fast acceleration and last-minute braking. It uses graphics to give tips on how to improve these habits and consequently how to lower insurance premiums. Controllers can use similar visual techniques to explain how a past decision has been made. 2. Article 15(1) (h) - Right of access Article 15(1) (h) entitles data subjects to have the same information about solely automated decision- making, including profiling, as required under Articles 13(2) (f) and 14(2) (g), namely:  the existence of automated decision making, including profiling;  meaningful information about the logic involved; and  the significance and envisaged consequences of such processing for the data subject. 41 Council of Europe. Draft Explanatory Report on the modernised version of CoE Convention 108, paragraph 75: “Data subjects should be entitled to know the reasoning underlying the processing of their data, including the consequences of such a reasoning, which led to any resulting conclusions, in particular in cases involving the use of algorithms for automated-decision making including profiling. For instance in the case of credit scoring, they should be entitled to know the logic underpinning the processing of their data and resulting in a ‘yes’ or ‘no’ decision, and not simply information on the decision itself. Without an understanding of these elements there could be no effective exercise of other essential safeguards such as the right to object and the right to complain to a competent authority.” https://rm.coe.int/CoERMPublicCommonSearchServices/DisplayDCTMContent?documentId=09000016806b6e c2 . Accessed 24 April 2017 27 The controller should have already given the data subject this information in line with their Article 13 obligations.42 Article 15(1)(h) says that the controller should provide the data subject with information about the envisaged consequences of the processing, rather than an explanation of a particular decision. Recital 63 clarifies this by stating that every data subject should have the right of access to obtain ‘communication’ about automatic data processing, including the logic involved, and at least when based on profiling, the consequences of such processing, By exercising their Article 15 rights, the data subject can become aware of a decision made concerning him or her, including one based on profiling. The controller should provide the data subject with general information (notably, on factors taken into account for the decision-making process, and on their respective ‘weight’ on an aggregate level) which is also useful for him or her to challenge the decision. F. Establishing appropriate safeguards If the basis for processing is 22(2)(a) or 22(2)(c), Article 22(3) requires controllers to implement suitable measures to safeguard data subjects’ rights freedoms and legitimate interests. Under Article 22(2)(b) the Member or Union State law that authorises the processing must also incorporate appropriate safeguarding measures. Such measures should include as a minimum a way for the data subject to obtain human intervention, express their point of view, and contest the decision. Human intervention is a key element. Any review must be carried out by someone who has the appropriate authority and capability to change the decision. The reviewer should undertake a thorough assessment of all the relevant data, including any additional information provided by the data subject. Recital 71 highlights that in any case suitable safeguards should also include: .. specific information to the data subject and the right ………… to obtain an explanation of the decision reached after such assessment and to challenge the decision. The controller must provide a simple way for the data subject to exercise these rights. This emphasises the need for transparency about the processing. [concept] Article 7 DMA — Article 7 of the DMA requires gatekeepers designated in relation to certain criteria.
Article 7 DMA requires gatekeepers to offer interoperability to alternative service providers for number-independent interpersonal communications services. It also addresses privacy and data protection concerns.
Article 7 DMA is a legal provision under the Digital Markets Act concerning interoperability requests.

[organization] Official Authorities — Official Authorities are entities that process personal data for aims laid down by constitutional law or international public law, particularly for officially recognized religious associations.
[organization] Union (European Union) — The Union refers to the European Union, which has an important public interest.
[person] Auditor — An auditor, who can be mandated by the controller, conducts inspections/audits to verify compliance.
[person] Joint Representative — An individual or entity appointed within a Member State when more than one supervisory authority is responsible for monitoring the application of the regulation.
[event] Merger — A change in legal and factual circumstances, such as the combining of the third-party undertaking with another company.

[concept] Restrictions — Limitations on the scope of obligations and rights provided in certain GDPR articles, applied under specific conditions listed in Article 23 GDPR.
Limitations applied to the scope of obligations and rights provided for in the GDPR, permissible under certain conditions detailed in Article 23.
Restrictions are limitations that can be imposed by Union or Member State law on specific principles and rights related to personal data processing, provided they are necessary and proportionate in a democratic society.
Legislative measures that may limit the scope of obligations and rights related to data protection under certain conditions.

[organization] Lead Authority — The lead authority is responsible for receiving notifications in the case of a cross-border personal data breach.
The Lead Authority is the relevant supervisory authority in the case of a cross-border personal data breach.
A supervisory authority identified to handle processing activities, especially for multinational companies or groups of undertakings based on decision-making centers.
The Lead Authority is a supervisory authority competent to adopt binding decisions in cases where a controller or processor is established in multiple Member States or substantially affects data subjects in more than one Member State.
The Lead Authority is a supervisory authority competent to adopt binding decisions and coordinate with other supervisory authorities in cases involving more than one Member State.
The lead authority is a supervisory authority whose draft decisions can be subject to objections from other supervisory authorities, as per Article 60(4).

[organization] Lead Supervisory Authority — The Lead Supervisory Authority (LSA) is a central concept within data protection under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), specifically designed for cross-border processing activities. Also recognized as the "one-stop-shop" mechanism, the LSA is the supervisory authority situated in the Member State of a controller's or processor's main establishment. Its primary role is to lead GDPR enforcement for cases that span multiple Member States, acting as the sole point of contact for the controller or processor in such multi-jurisdictional data protection scenarios. The LSA has a leading function in cooperation procedures, explicitly mandated to collaborate with other Concerned Supervisory Authorities (CSAs). Its responsibilities include overseeing data protection operations, conducting investigations, and sharing relevant information with other CSAs. The LSA is also tasked with preparing and submitting draft decisions under Articles 60 and 62, striving for consensus. It plays a crucial role in the Article 60 procedure. Furthermore, the LSA is responsible for handling objections raised by other CSAs. Should a dispute arise where the LSA does not follow a relevant and reasoned objection, or if it deems an objection not relevant or reasoned, it refers the matter to the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) for resolution. The LSA also conducts joint operations, facilitates information exchange, and serves as the primary authority a controller must notify in the event of a cross-border processing breach. As specified in GDPR Article 56, the LSA is a specific supervisory authority for transnational code members, underscoring its integral role in managing cross-border data protection and ensuring compliance, including taking measures intended to produce legal effects like administrative fines.
The Lead Supervisory Authority is the primary interlocutor for the Commission in cases where a gatekeeper has a "main establishment" under the GDPR, responsible for cooperating with concerned supervisory authorities.

[method] Anonymisation — Anonymisation is a fundamental process and set of techniques designed to transform personal data in such a way that the data subject can no longer be identified, either directly or indirectly. This alteration ensures that the data cannot be linked back to an individual, even with reasonable effort. It is employed when personal data is deleted or anonymized following the withdrawal of consent, acting as a crucial safeguard in data processing, particularly for research purposes where the objective can be achieved without handling identifiable data. This process involves carefully processing data, such as location information, to prevent re-identification, a task that is complex and necessitates transparency regarding the methodologies used. The goal of anonymisation extends beyond simply removing identifying information to also preserving the quality and usefulness of the data for its intended purpose. It is a preferred solution and safeguard, recognized by regulations like the Digital Markets Act (DMA), specifically Article 6(11), and the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), which require the rendering of personal data anonymous to protect end-user privacy. In essence, anonymisation serves as a vital method for making shared personal data suitable for use while upholding the privacy of individuals.
Anonymization is a method by which shared data that constitutes personal data is processed to remove identifying information.
Anonymisation is a method of altering personal data so that the data subject is no longer identifiable, with the goal of preserving data quality and usefulness.
Anonymisation is the process of altering personal data to prevent direct or indirect identification of end users, required for data shared under Article 6(11) DMA.
Anonymisation is the process of rendering personal data anonymous to protect end-user privacy, as required by DMA and GDPR.

Under GDPR, Anonymisation Gatekeepers are responsible for selecting the appropriate anonymisation methods for end-user data. Gatekeeper.

Under GDPR, Controller The relationship between a Controller and a Lead Supervisory Authority is central to the oversight of cross-border data processing, particularly under regulations such as the GDPR. The Lead Supervisory Authority is identified for a Controller primarily based on its central administration or the location where decisions concerning processing are made. In situations involving both a Controller and a Processor, the Lead Supervisory Authority for the Controller typically assumes the role for both, as outlined in Recital 36. Furthermore, the supervisory authority of a Member State where a Controller's main establishment is located often acts as the competent Lead Supervisory Authority. This authority is competent to supervise cross-border processing activities conducted by a Controller. A key responsibility of the Controller in this relationship is to notify the Lead Supervisory Authority in the event of breaches involving cross-border processing. Beyond notification, the Lead Supervisory Authority serves as the sole interlocutor for the Controller regarding cross-border processing, meaning the Controller interacts exclusively with this authority. The Lead Supervisory Authority is responsible for adopting and notifying decisions to the Controller, who, in turn, is obligated to take measures to ensure compliance with these decisions. Lead Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Controller A controller may or may not be established in the Union, which determines certain aspects of this Regulation's applicability. The Union.
Under GDPR, Auditor The processor must make information available and contribute to audits conducted by the auditor. Processor.

Under GDPR, Controller In the context of the GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation), a Controller is defined as the entity that determines the purposes and means of processing personal data. This role is fundamental to the GDPR framework, outlining significant obligations and responsibilities for any party involved in deciding how personal data is processed. Controllers are accountable for ensuring compliance with all GDPR provisions, particularly as detailed in Article 3. The Controller has legal obligations to ensure compliance with the GDPR and is responsible and liable under its provisions, with these obligations often referred to as "GDPR controller obligations." The responsibilities of a Controller include applying GDPR rules to data processing, adhering to foundational principles like lawfulness and fairness (Article 5(1)), and fulfilling transparency obligations. Specific duties extend to maintaining records of processing activities (Article 30), ensuring the security of processing (Article 32), conducting data protection impact assessments (Article 35), and engaging in prior consultation (Article 36) for managing risks. Controllers must also comply with regulations regarding data storage periods, implement appropriate technical and organizational measures, and adhere to specific legal bases, such as legitimate interest, for processing data. The GDPR places a significant burden of proof on the Controller, requiring them to demonstrate "compelling legitimate grounds for the processing" when applicable. They must ensure consent mechanisms meet GDPR standards and act upon withdrawal requests. Compliance with obligations related to accountability, engaging processors, and maintaining data security is paramount. Regarding data breaches, the GDPR mandates that controllers implement appropriate security measures, ensure compliance with processing principles, and adhere to relevant provisions for breach notification, as specified in Article 60(10). Unless a breach is unlikely to result in a risk to individuals' rights and freedoms, the Controller is required to notify the competent supervisory authority and, in certain cases, the affected individuals. The GDPR further defines notification and communication requirements for Controllers regarding breaches, and they must retain documentation in accordance with Article 33(5) of the GDPR. The GDPR also places the burden of proof regarding objections on the Controller. Supervisory Authorities are empowered to take enforcement action, including imposing fines, for any infringement of GDPR provisions resulting from a Controller's actions. The ability for data controllers to obtain GDPR certifications further underscores their pivotal role and accountability within the regulatory framework, though some Controllers currently face broad and systemic issues in compliance with the GDPR. The GDPR recognizes that Controllers may not have all information immediately and allows for phased notification.<SEP>Controllers must comply with the provisions of the GDPR, including those related to information provision.<SEP>The controller must comply with GDPR requirements, including those related to transparency and notification.<SEP>A controller's responsibilities are defined under the GDPR. GDPR.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller must cease other processing activities while retaining personal data when a restriction is in place. Right to Restriction of Processing.
Under GDPR, Lead Authority The Lead Authority is a type of Supervisory Authority that coordinates with other Supervisory Authorities in decision-making processes. Supervisory Authority.

Under GDPR, Competent Data Protection Supervisory Authorities The European Commission coordinates, cooperates, and consults with competent data protection supervisory authorities. European Commission.
Under GDPR, Data Protection Training Appropriate data protection training is required for personnel having permanent or regular access to personal data. Personnel.
Under GDPR, Gatekeepers Gatekeepers should keep an internal list of all categories of data that can be ported under Article 6(9) DMA. Internal List of Data Categories.
Under GDPR, Controller Controllers should verify the identity of a data subject requests access, particularly in context of online services. Online Services.
Under GDPR, Merger The enforceability of measures imposed by the implementing act should remain even after a merger of the third party undertaking. Third-Party Undertaking.
Under GDPR, CPS A hyperlink to the dashboard for data recipients may be included in the CPS's privacy notice. Privacy Notice.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Restriction of processing applies to personal data. Restriction of Processing.
Under GDPR, Commission M. Schulz, as President of the European Parliament, represents one of the parties for which the Regulation was done. M. Schulz.
Under GDPR, Economic Union Regulation (EU) 2016/679 is intended to contribute to the accomplishment of an economic union. Regulation (EU) 2016/679.

Under GDPR, Controller The relation "(Controller, Personal Data)" signifies that the Controller is the entity that determines the purposes and means of processing personal data. This involves significant authority and responsibility over the entire lifecycle of personal data, encompassing decisions related to its processing, protection, management, and transference. The Controller is explicitly responsible for establishing the retention period for documentation containing personal data and makes choices regarding its deletion or return. A core obligation of the Controller is to ensure compliance with data protection regulations and to uphold data subject rights, including guaranteeing data accuracy and facilitating the exercise of those rights. The Controller is always required to have an appropriate lawful ground for the processing of personal data, and while explicit consent may be obtained, it is not always the sole lawful basis, especially when processing is essential for a contract. The Controller is also responsible for the overall protection and security of personal data, which includes managing and responding to any data breaches, and applying appropriate technical and organizational measures to safeguard it. This responsibility extends to implementing measures to minimize the processing of personal data. In addition to determining purposes and means, the Controller collects, processes, and stores personal data. The Controller may also determine its identification requirements and, in the EU, has the capability to make personal data accessible to a third-country authority, and may specifically process data indicating criminal acts when considered within their legitimate interest. Persons under the direct authority of the Controller are authorized to process personal data. The Controller is ultimately responsible and liable for any processing of personal data, including its transferring. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Processor A processor may or may not be established in the Union, which determines certain aspects of this Regulation's applicability. The Union.

Under GDPR, GDPR The Lead Supervisory Authority leads the enforcement of the GDPR for cross-border processing activities.<SEP>The GDPR provides for shared responsibility in monitoring its application, influencing the LSA's position. Lead Supervisory Authority.

Under GDPR, Controller The controller must implement restriction of processing under specified conditions and verify data accuracy.<SEP>The controller must communicate any restriction of processing. Restriction of Processing.
Under GDPR, Direct Marketing This judgment provides legal precedent for the notion of direct marketing. StWL Städtische Werke Lauf a.d. Pegnitz v eprimo GmbH, C-102/20.

Under GDPR, Consent Pursuant to Directive 95/46/EC Processing based on consent under Directive 95/46/EC does not require new consent if it aligns with this Regulation's conditions. Processing Already Under Way.

Under GDPR, Controller The controller provides contact details for the controller's representative.<SEP>The record of processing activities for a controller may include details of the controller's representative. Controller's Representative.

Under GDPR, Commission The Commission considers developments in information technology when proposing amendments to the Regulation. Information Technology.

The shared data that constitutes personal data has to be anonymised. The Guidelines clarify the objective of Article 6(11) DMA to foster contestability in the online search engine market and how to achieve effective anonymisation of shared search data while taking into account such objectives. Article 7 DMA requires gatekeepers designated in relation to their number-independent interpersonal communications services to offer interoperability to alternative service providers requesting it. The Guidelines elaborate on the requirements of Article 7 that are relevant from the perspective of privacy and data protection law. Most notably, they recall that gatekeepers should comply with data minimisation and other GDPR principles when making their services interoperable, and mention necessary, proportionate, and justified measures that a gatekeeper may apply to ensure that third-party service providers requesting interoperability do not endanger the integrity, security and privacy of its services. The Guidelines also pronounce on coordination, cooperation and consultation between the European Commission as sole enforcer of the DMA and competent data protection supervisory authorities as enforcers of the GDPR. The Guidelines recall, with reference to relevant CJEU case law concerning the principle of sincere cooperation and ne bis in idem, that cooperation and coordination between the European Commission
